items.title: Spotlight on SIG Apps
items.content:
In our ongoing SIG Spotlight series, we dive into the heart of the Kubernetes project by talking to the leaders of its various Special Interest Groups (SIGs). This time, we focus on , the group responsible for everything related to developing, deploying, and operating applications on Kubernetes. Sandipan Panda (DevZero) had the opportunity to interview Maciej Szulik (Defense Unicorns) and Janet Kuo (Google), the chairs and tech leads of SIG Apps. They shared their experiences, challenges, and visions for the future of application management within the Kubernetes ecosystem.

Sandipan: Hello, could you start by telling us a bit about yourself, your role, and your journey within the Kubernetes community that led to your current roles in SIG Apps?

: Hey, my name is Maciej, and I’m one of the leads for SIG Apps. Aside from this role, you can also find me helping SIG CLI and also being one of the Steering Committee members. I’ve been contributing to Kubernetes since late 2014 in various areas, including controllers, apiserver, and kubectl.

: Certainly! I'm Janet, a Staff Software Engineer at Google, and I've been deeply involved with the Kubernetes project since its early days, even before the 1.0 launch in 2015. It's been an amazing journey!

My current role within the Kubernetes community is one of the chairs and tech leads of SIG Apps. My journey with SIG Apps started organically. I started with building the Deployment API and adding rolling update functionalities. I naturally gravitated towards SIG Apps and became increasingly involved. Over time, I took on more responsibilities, culminating in my current leadership roles.

All following answers were jointly provided by Maciej and Janet.

Sandipan: For those unfamiliar, could you provide an overview of SIG Apps' mission and objectives? What key problems does it aim to solve within the Kubernetes ecosystem?

As described in our charter, we cover a broad area related to developing, deploying, and operating applications on Kubernetes. That, in short, means we’re open to each and everyone showing up at our bi-weekly meetings and discussing the ups and downs of writing and deploying various applications on Kubernetes.

Sandipan: What are some of the most significant projects or initiatives currently being undertaken by SIG Apps?

At this point in time, the main factors driving the development of our controllers are the challenges coming from running various AI-related workloads. It’s worth giving credit here to two working groups we’ve sponsored over the past years:

Best practices and challenges

Sandipan: SIG Apps plays a crucial role in developing application management best practices for Kubernetes. Can you share some of these best practices and how they help improve application lifecycle management?

  1. Implementing health checks and readiness probes ensures that your applications are healthy and ready to serve traffic, leading to improved reliability and uptime. The above, combined with comprehensive logging, monitoring, and tracing solutions, will provide insights into your application's behavior, enabling you to identify and resolve issues quickly.

  2. Auto-scale your application based on resource utilization or custom metrics, optimizing resource usage and ensuring your application can handle varying loads.

  3. Use Deployment for stateless applications, StatefulSet for stateful applications, Job and CronJob for batch workloads, and DaemonSet for running a daemon on each node. Use Operators and CRDs to extend the Kubernetes API to automate the deployment, management, and lifecycle of complex applications, making them easier to operate and reducing manual intervention.

Sandipan: What are some of the common challenges SIG Apps faces, and how do you address them?

The biggest challenge we’re facing all the time is the need to reject a lot of features, ideas, and improvements. This requires a lot of discipline and patience to be able to explain the reasons behind those decisions.

Sandipan: How has the evolution of Kubernetes influenced the work of SIG Apps? Are there any recent changes or upcoming features in Kubernetes that you find particularly relevant or beneficial for SIG Apps?

The main benefit for both us and the whole community around SIG Apps is the ability to extend kubernetes with Custom Resource Definitions and the fact that users can build their own custom controllers leveraging the built-in ones to achieve whatever sophisticated use cases they might have and we, as the core maintainers, haven’t considered or weren’t able to efficiently resolve inside Kubernetes.

Sandipan: What opportunities are available for new contributors who want to get involved with SIG Apps, and what advice would you give them?

We get the question, "What good first issue might you recommend we start with?" a lot :-) But unfortunately, there’s no easy answer to it. We always tell everyone that the best option to start contributing to core controllers is to find one you are willing to spend some time with. Read through the code, then try running unit tests and integration tests focusing on that controller. Once you grasp the general idea, try breaking it and the tests again to verify your breakage. Once you start feeling confident you understand that particular controller, you may want to search through open issues affecting that controller and either provide suggestions, explaining the problem users have, or maybe attempt your first fix.

Like we said, there are no shortcuts on that road; you need to spend the time with the codebase to understand all the edge cases we’ve slowly built up to get to the point where we are. Once you’re successful with one controller, you’ll need to repeat that same process with others all over again.

Sandipan: How does SIG Apps gather feedback from the community, and how is this feedback integrated into your work?

We always encourage everyone to show up and present their problems and solutions during our bi-weekly meetings. As long as you’re solving an interesting problem on top of Kubernetes and you can provide valuable feedback about any of the core controllers, we’re always happy to hear from everyone.

Sandipan: Looking ahead, what are the key focus areas or upcoming trends in application management within Kubernetes that SIG Apps is excited about? How is the SIG adapting to these trends?

Definitely the current AI hype is the major driving factor; as mentioned above, we have two working groups, each covering a different aspect of it.

Sandipan: What are some of your favorite things about this SIG?

Without a doubt, the people that participate in our meetings and on Slack, who tirelessly help triage issues, pull requests and invest a lot of their time (very frequently their private time) into making kubernetes great!

SIG Apps is an essential part of the Kubernetes community, helping to shape how applications are deployed and managed at scale. From its work on improving Kubernetes' workload APIs to driving innovation in AI/ML application management, SIG Apps is continually adapting to meet the needs of modern application developers and operators. Whether you’re a new contributor or an experienced developer, there’s always an opportunity to get involved and make an impact.

If you’re interested in learning more or contributing to SIG Apps, be sure to check out their SIG README and join their bi-weekly meetings.

items.title: Announcing the end of support for Node.js 14.x and 16.x in AWS CDK
items.author: Adam Keller
items.content:
On May 30th, 2025, the AWS Cloud Development Kit (CDK) will no longer support Node.js 14.x and 16.x, which reached end of life on 4/30/2023 (14.x) and 9/11/2023 (16.x). This change applies to all AWS CDK components that depend on Node.js, including the AWS CDK CLI, the Construct Library, and broader CDK ecosystem projects such as JSII, Projen, and CDK8s.

Node.js 14x and 16.x are past their End of Life and are no longer supported by the Node.js community. This means that there have not been any bug fixes or security updates to these versions. To make sure that we are providing up-to-date and secure libraries, we will drop support for these versions.

After , the AWS CDK will no longer support Node.js 14.x and 16.x. While your existing deployments may continue to work, we will not address issues specific to these versions. Any bug reports or support cases that stem from using Node.js 14.x or 16.x will require reproducing the issue on a supported version of Node.js (18.x, 20.x, 22.x – as of February 26th, 2025) before further assistance can be provided.

  • New features for the AWS CDK may rely on APIs or functionalities only available in supported versions of Node.js.
  • Critical security patches and fixes related to Node.js 14.x or 16.x will not be backported.
  • Compatibility testing will no longer be performed for Node.js 16.x, making it difficult to guarantee the CDK’s behavior with that runtime.
  1. March 11, 2025 through May 30, 2025
    1. The AWS CDK is officially dropping support for Node.js 14.x and 16.x.
    2. Any bug fixes or security patches will target only supported versions of Node.js (18.x, 20.x, 22.x – as of March 11th, 2025)

Version validation and update steps

  • Check your current Node.js version
    • Run node -v in your environment or CI/CD pipelines to see which version of Node.js you’re currently using.
    • Install or switch your runtime to Node.js using a supported version via a version manager (e.g., nvm) or by downloading an official installer from nodejs.org.
  • Validate your AWS CDK projects
    • Ensure your deployment scripts, and any third-party dependencies work correctly under a supported version. Test thoroughly in non-production environments.
    • For more information on our deprecation strategy moving forward, please see this RFC, which provides more details.

This deprecation is part of our ongoing commitment to provide a secure, high-quality experience for AWS CDK users. By moving to a Node.js Active Long Term Support (LTS) version, you’ll benefit from improved performance, ongoing security patches, and continued AWS CDK innovations. If you have any questions or concerns about this deprecation, please reach out and open an issue in our GitHub repo.

items.title: Kubernetes Secrets vs ConfigMaps — A Detailed Guide
items.author: Ramksai
items.content:
Kubernetes Secrets vs ConfigMaps — A Detailed Guide

In Kubernetes, and are resources used to manage external configuration and sensitive data separately from application code. This separation improves security, flexibility, and maintainability by ensuring that applications can be configured dynamically without modifying container images.

  • store non-sensitive data such as application settings, environment variables, and configuration files.
  • store sensitive data like database credentials, API tokens, and SSH keys securely.

This document covers what they are, where and why they are used, the risks of storing credentials in ConfigMaps, how Kubernetes stores Secrets, and how to use them in Deployments.

A is a Kubernetes object used to store configuration data . It helps keep environment-specific settings separate from application code, ensuring better portability.

Key Features of ConfigMap:

* Stores configuration data.* Can be used as environment variables, volume mounts, or command-line arguments.* Allows applications to be configured dynamically without requiring redeployment.* Supports storing key-value pairs, YAML, or JSON data.

Example ConfigMap Definition (YAML)

apiVersion: v1kind: ConfigMap  name: app-config  APP_ENV: "production"  DB_HOST: "postgres-service"

A is a Kubernetes resource designed to store and manage securely. Unlike ConfigMaps, Secrets are and can be encrypted using Kubernetes .

* Used for storing such as passwords, API keys, and certificates.* Encoded in to prevent accidental exposure.* Supports using Kubernetes Key Management Service (KMS).* Access is restricted to authorized Pods only.

Example Secret Definition (YAML)

apiVersion: v1kind: Secret  name: db-secretdata:
  DB_USERNAME: dXNlcm5hbWU=  # "username" in base64
  DB_PASSWORD: cGFzc3dvcmQ=  # "password" in base64

Where are ConfigMaps and Secrets Used?

Both are used for configuring applications dynamically. They are commonly used in:

Why Do We Need to Use ConfigMaps and Secrets?

Using provides the following benefits:

  1.  — Configuration and sensitive data are managed independently of application code.
  2.  — Sensitive credentials are stored securely in Secrets rather than hardcoding them in application code.
  3.  — Applications can be updated or modifying container images.
  4.  — ConfigMaps and Secrets allow easy migration between different environments.

What Happens If Credentials Are Used in ConfigMaps?

Using credentials in ConfigMaps is a bad security practice because:

* ConfigMaps are stored as plain text in etcd (Kubernetes’ database).* Any user with access to the cluster can read the credentials.

* Logs and debugging outputs can expose credentials.

* No encryption or access control is applied by default.

Example of Storing Credentials in a ConfigMap (BAD PRACTICE)

apiVersion: v1kind: ConfigMap  name: insecure-config  DB_PASSWORD: "my-secret-password"

* Instead, use to store sensitive data.

How Are Credentials Stored in Kubernetes Secrets?

Kubernetes Secrets store data but are .

Steps for Encoding Secrets

Convert a string to base64:

echo -n "my-password" | base64# Output: bXktcGFzc3dvcmQ=

Store it in a Secret YAML:

apiVersion: v1kind: Secret  name: secure-secretdata:
  password: bXktcGFzc3dvcmQ=
echo "bXktcGFzc3dvcmQ=" | base64 --decode# Output: my-password
  • : Enable for Secrets in Kubernetes to ensure .

How to Use ConfigMaps and Secrets in a Deployment?

ConfigMaps and Secrets can be injected into a Pod as:- -

Using ConfigMap and Secret as Environment Variables in a Deployment

apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment  name: app  replicas: 1    matchLabels:  template:      labels:    spec:        - name: my-container          env:              valueFrom:                  name: app-config            - name: DB_USERNAME                secretKeyRef:                  key: DB_USERNAME              valueFrom:                  name: db-secret

Mounting ConfigMaps and Secrets as Volumes

apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment  name: app1  replicas: 1    matchLabels:  template:      labels:    spec:        - name: config-volume            name: app-config          secret:      containers:          image: nginx            - name: config-volume            - name: secret-volume

Access the data inside the container:

kubectl exec -it <app-pod-name> -- cat /etc/config/APP_ENVkubectl exec -it <app1-pod-name> -- cat /etc/secret/DB_PASSWORD

Summary of Best Practices

* Use for storing credentials instead of ConfigMaps.* Enable for Secrets for .* Use RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) to restrict access to Secrets. * Use Immutable ConfigMaps and Secrets to prevent accidental changes.* commit Secrets or ConfigMaps in Git repositories.

items.title: Introduction to StorageClass and PVC in Kubernetes
items.author: Ramksai
items.content:
Persistent Volumes (PV) Persistent Volume Claims (PVC) are core components of Kubernetes designed to manage storage needs for applications running in a containerized environment. They ensure that data remains available and persistent, even if the underlying containers or pods are restarted, scaled, or replaced.What is this?

  • Persistent Volume (PV): A Persistent Volume is a storage resource in a Kubernetes cluster that provides a way to store data independently of the lifecycle of pods. It can be backed by various storage types, such as local disks, cloud storage (e.g., AWS EBS, Google Persistent Disk), or network file systems (NFS).
  • Persistent Volume Claim (PVC): A Persistent Volume Claim is a request made by an application for storage. It specifies the amount of storage needed and the access mode (e.g., read/write). The PVC is then matched to an available PV, enabling the application to use the requested storage.
  • : A blueprint for dynamic PV provisioning. Defines and storage is provisioned (e.g., gp3 volumes on AWS). StorageClasses automate PV creation when a PVC is submitted.

PersistentVolumeClaims are requests for storage resources by users or applications. They abstract the details of storage implementation from the users, allowing them to request storage without knowing the underlying infrastructure. StorageClasses, on the other hand, define different types of storage offered in a cluster, enabling dynamic provisioning of Persistent Volumes (PVs)

  1. Dynamic Provisioning: When a PVC requests storage with a specific StorageClass, Kubernetes can automatically create a PV that matches the requirements3.
  2. Storage Types: StorageClasses allow administrators to offer various storage options (e.g., SSDs, HDDs) optimized for different workloads1.
  3. Access Modes: PVCs can specify how the storage should be accessed (e.g., ReadWriteOnce, ReadOnlyMany)1.

Your examples are accurate. Here’s an expanded view with additional scenarios:

  • Databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB)
  • Message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ)
  • Content Management Systems (WordPress, Drupal)

Example: An e-commerce platform uses a PVC with a high-performance StorageClass for its product database, ensuring fast read/write operations even during traffic spikes2.

  • Big data processing (Hadoop, Spark)
  • Machine Learning model storage

Example: A data science team uses PVCs with a StorageClass optimized for large file storage to persist machine learning models and datasets4.

3. Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD):

    Example: A CI/CD pipeline uses PVCs to store build artifacts and caches, improving build times and ensuring consistency across different build environments5.

      Example: A video streaming service uses PVCs with a StorageClass that provides high throughput for storing and processing large media files2.

      5. Logging and Monitoring:

      • Time-series databases for metrics

      Example: An organization uses PVCs with a StorageClass that supports high write throughput for storing application logs and monitoring data from multiple microservices5.

      6. Backup and Disaster Recovery:

        Example: A financial institution uses PVCs with a StorageClass that supports snapshots for point-in-time recovery of critical financial data4.

        By leveraging PVCs and StorageClasses, organizations can ensure data persistence, optimize storage performance for specific workloads, and simplify storage management in Kubernetes environments. This approach allows for better resource utilization, improved application reliability, and easier scaling of storage resources as needs change

        1. Data Persistence: Ensures that critical application data remains intact even if containers are restarted, replaced, or scaled.
        2. Separation of Storage from Pods: Decouples storage from the ephemeral nature of containers, enabling better resource management.
        3. Flexibility: Supports various backend storage solutions (local disks, cloud-based storage, network-attached storage).
        4. Scalability: Dynamically provisions storage as application needs grow.
        5. Reliability: Provides a stable mechanism for managing stateful workloads in Kubernetes.
        6. Improved User Experience: Ensures seamless operation for end-users by preventing data loss during disruptions caused by traffic surges, resource limitations, or dependency failures.

        By using PV and SC in Kubernetes, organizations can build robust and reliable systems capable of handling real-world challenges while ensuring data integrity and availability at all times.

        To prepare AWS and EKS to use Amazon EBS volumes with the EBS CSI Driver and configure IAM roles using IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts), follow these steps:

        Step 1: Add the EBS CSI Driver Add-on to Your EKS Cluster

        The Amazon EBS CSI Driver allows Kubernetes workloads running on EKS to use Amazon EBS volumes as persistent storage.

        • Ensure your EKS cluster is running Kubernetes version 1.20 or later.
        • Install the required tools: eksctl, kubectl, and the AWS CLI.

        Step 1: Add the EBS CSI Driver Add-on

        You can add the driver using eksctl or the AWS Management Console.

        We are installing the Amazon EBS CSI Driver add-on in our Amazon EKS cluster. This add-on enables Kubernetes to dynamically provision and manage Amazon EBS volumes as Persistent Volumes (PVs) for containerized workloads. By installing the add-on, we allow Kubernetes to interact with Amazon EBS storage resources seamlessly.The commands provided (eksctl and AWS CLI) configure the EBS CSI driver and associate it with an IAM role that grants the necessary permissions to manage EBS volumes.

        Why Are We Adding the Add-on?

        The EBS CSI Driver add-on is required because Kubernetes does not natively support direct integration with Amazon EBS. The CSI (Container Storage Interface) driver acts as a bridge between Kubernetes and Amazon EBS, enabling Kubernetes to perform storage operations like:

        1. Dynamic Volume Provisioning: Automatically create and attach EBS volumes when a Persistent Volume Claim (PVC) is submitted.
        2. Volume Attachment/Detachment: Attach or detach EBS volumes to/from pods as needed.
        3. Volume Deletion: Automatically delete unused volumes if configured.
        4. Storage Management: Handle advanced features like volume resizing and encryption.

        Without this add-on, Kubernetes cannot interact with Amazon EBS, and you would need to manually provision and manage storage outside of Kubernetes, which is inefficient and error-prone.

        eksctl create addon \  --name aws-ebs-csi-driver \
          --cluster <your-cluster-name> \
          --service-account-role-arn arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole \
        aws eks create-addon \  --cluster-name <your-cluster-name> \
          --addon-name aws-ebs-csi-driver \
          --service-account-role-arn arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole

        Step 2: Enable IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA)

        Enabling IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA) is a critical step in securely managing access to AWS resources from applications running in Kubernetes pods.

        In this step, we are enabling IRSA by associating an OIDC (OpenID Connect) Identity Provider with our Amazon EKS cluster. This allows Kubernetes service accounts to assume specific IAM roles, granting pods fine-grained access to AWS resources like Amazon EBS, S3, DynamoDB, or others.

        Create an OIDC Identity Provider

        1. Verify if your cluster has an OIDC provider:
        aws eks describe-cluster --name <your-cluster-name> --query "cluster.identity.oidc.issuer" --output text

        2. If no OIDC provider exists, create one using eksctl:

        eksctl utils associate-iam-oidc-provider \   --region <region> \   --cluster <your-cluster-name> \   --approve

        Step 3: Create an IAM Role and Policy for the EBS CSI Driver

        In this step, we are creating an IAM Policy and an IAM Role to grant the EBS CSI Driver the necessary permissions to manage Amazon EBS volumes. Additionally, we configure a trust relationship to allow the Kubernetes service account (ebs-csi-controller-sa) in your EKS cluster to assume this role securely.

        • This policy defines the specific actions that the EBS CSI driver is allowed to perform on AWS resources (e.g., creating, attaching, detaching, and deleting EBS volumes).
        • The policy is attached to an IAM role that will be assumed by the Kubernetes service account used by the EBS CSI driver.

        2. Creating an IAM Role with a Trust Relationship:

        • The IAM role includes a trust relationship that allows your cluster’s OIDC provider (via IRSA) to assume the role on behalf of the Kubernetes service account.
        • This ensures that only authenticated requests from your cluster can use this role.

        1. Granting Permissions for EBS Management

        The EBS CSI driver is responsible for managing Amazon EBS volumes in your Kubernetes cluster. It needs permissions to:

        • Dynamically provision new EBS volumes when a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) is created.
        • Attach and detach volumes to/from EC2 instances hosting Kubernetes pods.
        • Delete unused volumes when they are no longer needed (if configured with a Delete reclaim policy).
        • Describe existing volumes and EC2 instances for monitoring and operational purposes.

        Without these permissions, the EBS CSI driver cannot function, and Kubernetes workloads requiring persistent storage will fail.

        2. Enabling Secure Authentication via IRSA

        By using IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts), we:

        • Avoid granting broad permissions at the node level (via instance profiles), which could be misused by other pods running on the same node.
        • Scope permissions specifically to the service account (ebs-csi-controller-sa) used by the EBS CSI driver.
        • Use short-lived tokens issued by the OIDC provider for authentication, improving security.

        3. Following Principle of Least Privilege

        The custom policy ensures that only the required actions are allowed. For example:

        • ec2:CreateVolume allows provisioning of new storage.
        • ec2:AttachVolume and ec2:DetachVolume handle mounting/unmounting of storage to/from pods.
        • ec2:DescribeVolumes and ec2:DescribeInstances are needed for operational visibility.

        This minimizes risks by ensuring that no unnecessary permissions are granted.

        4. Enabling Dynamic Storage Management

        The IAM role enables Kubernetes workloads to dynamically provision and manage persistent storage using Amazon EBS without requiring manual intervention or static credentials.

        Step 1: Create an IAM Policy

        The EBS CSI Driver requires specific permissions to manage EBS volumes. Use the AWS-managed policy AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicy or create a custom policy.

        {    "Version": "2012-10-17",        {            "Action": [                "ec2:AttachVolume",                "ec2:DetachVolume",                "ec2:DescribeInstances"            "Resource": "*"    ]

        Save the above JSON file as ebs-csi-policy.json and create it using:

        aws iam create-policy \  --policy-name AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicy \
          --policy-document file://ebs-csi-policy.json

        Step 2: Create an IAM Role

        The IAM role allows the Kubernetes service account (ebs-csi-controller-sa) to assume it via IRSA. This role is scoped specifically for managing Amazon EBS resources.

        {    "Version": "2012-10-17",        {            "Principal": {
                        "Federated": "arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:oidc-provider/<oidc-provider-url>"
                    },
                    "Action": "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity",
                    "Condition": {                    "<oidc-provider-url>:sub": "system:serviceaccount:kube-system:ebs-csi-controller-sa"            }    ]

        Save the trust relationship JSON as trust-relationship.json and create it using:

        aws iam create-role \  --role-name AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole \
          --assume-role-policy-document file://trust-relationship.json

        Step 3: Attach the Policy to the Role

        Attach the previously created policy (AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicy) to this role:

        aws iam attach-role-policy \  --role-name AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole \
          --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:policy/AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicy
        • Grants only the required permissions for managing Amazon EBS resources.
        • Allows authenticated requests from your cluster’s OIDC provider to assume this role securely.

        3. Configured a Trust Relationship:

        • Ensures that only authenticated service accounts in your cluster can use this IAM role, tying it specifically to ebs-csi-controller-sa.
        • Linked the custom policy (AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicy) with this role so that it has all necessary permissions.

        Step 4: Associate the IAM Role with Pod Identity

        Use the AWS CLI to associate the IAM role with the service account used by the EBS CSI driver (ebs-csi-controller-sa) via Pod Identity Associations.

        aws eks update-addon \  --cluster-name <your-cluster-name> \
          --addon-name aws-ebs-csi-driver \
          --pod-identity-associations 'serviceAccount=ebs-csi-controller-sa,namespace=kube-system,roleArn=arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole'
        • Replace <your-cluster-name> with your EKS cluster name.
        • Replace <account-id> with your AWS account ID.
        • Replace AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole with the name of your IAM role.

        This command updates the aws-ebs-csi-driver add-on and associates its service account (ebs-csi-controller-sa) in the kube-system namespace with your IAM role.

        Step 5: Validate Pod Identity Associations

        Once you’ve updated the add-on, verify that the Pod Identity Association has been created successfully.

        aws eks list-pod-identity-associations --cluster-name <your-cluster-name>

        You should see an entry like this

        [    {
                "namespace": "kube-system",
                "serviceAccount": "ebs-csi-controller-sa",
                "roleArn": "arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole"
            }

        This confirms that the service account is now associated with the specified IAM role using Pod Identity Associations.

        Step 5: Validate Installation

        1. Check that the EBS CSI Driver pods are running:
        kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep ebs

        2. Verify the storage classes available in your cluster:

        Step 6: Create StorageClass

        A StorageClass in Kubernetes defines how storage resources (like Amazon EBS volumes) are provisioned dynamically. By creating a StorageClass, we are enabling dynamic provisioning of storage for workloads that request PersistentVolumes (PVs). This eliminates the need to manually create and manage storage resources.In this specific case, we are using the Amazon EBS CSI driver (ebs.csi.aws.com) as the provisioner to dynamically manage EBS volumes in AWS.

        Why We Selected This Configuration

        1. Dynamic Volume Provisioning

        • The StorageClass allows Kubernetes to automatically provision Amazon EBS volumes when a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) is created.
        • This simplifies storage management, as you don’t need to pre-create EBS volumes manually in AWS.
        • It ensures that storage is provisioned only when required by workloads.

        2. Integration with Amazon EBS CSI Driver

        • The provisioner: ebs.csi.aws.com specifies that the Amazon EBS CSI driver will handle the provisioning and management of EBS volumes.
        • The EBS CSI driver is optimized for Kubernetes and supports advanced features like volume resizing, encryption, and snapshots.

        3. Efficient Volume Binding with WaitForFirstConsumer

        • volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer delays the provisioning and binding of the volume until a pod using the PVC is scheduled.
        • Ensures that the volume is created in the same availability zone as the pod, avoiding cross-zone data transfer costs and improving performance.
        • Useful in multi-AZ clusters where pods can be scheduled in different zones.
        1. A user or application creates a PVC specifying:
        • The amount of storage required (e.g., 5Gi).
        • The access mode (e.g., ReadWriteOnce).
        • The StorageClass name (ebs-sc).

        2. Kubernetes uses the StorageClass (ebs-sc) to dynamically create an Amazon EBS volume using the parameters defined in the StorageClass.

        3. The Amazon EBS CSI driver provisions the volume in AWS and binds it to the PVC.

        4. The PVC is then mounted to a pod, allowing applications to store and retrieve data from the volume.

        Role of Pod Identity Associations

        • Since you have already configured Pod Identity Associations using:
        aws eks update-addon \   --cluster-name <your-cluster-name> \
           --addon-name aws-ebs-csi-driver \
           --pod-identity-associations 'serviceAccount=ebs-csi-controller-sa,namespace=kube-system,roleArn=arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole'
        • The Amazon EBS CSI driver uses the IAM role associated with ebs-csi-controller-sa to interact with AWS services securely:
        • Create/Delete/Attach/Detach EBS volumes.
        • Ensure proper permissions without relying on node-level instance profiles.
        apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1kind: StorageClass  name: ebs-sc
        provisioner: ebs.csi.aws.com
        volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer  type: gp3

        Apply the above SC, here is the command to apply and check:

        kubectl apply -f sc.yaml

        Step 7: Create a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC)

        A PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) is a request for storage from a StorageClass in Kubernetes. It acts as an abstraction layer between the application and the underlying storage, enabling dynamic provisioning of storage resources like Amazon EBS volumes.

        In this step, we are creating a PVC that requests storage from the ebs-sc StorageClass (created earlier). This PVC specifies:

        1. The amount of storage needed (e.g., 5Gi).
        2. The access mode (e.g., ReadWriteOnce).
        3. The StorageClass to use (e.g., ebs-sc).
        apiVersion: v1kind: PersistentVolumeClaim  name: ebs-pvc  accessModes:
            - ReadWriteOnce # Only one pod can access at a time
          resources:      storage: 5Gi # Request 5GB of storage
          storageClassName: ebs-sc # Use the StorageClass created earlier

        Apply the above PvC, here I am apply the pvc in test ns:

        kubectl apply -f pvc.yaml -n test
        1. Dynamic Volume Provisioning:
        • The PVC triggers the dynamic provisioning of an Amazon EBS volume using the EBS CSI driver and the specified StorageClass.
        • Kubernetes automatically creates a PersistentVolume (PV) backed by an EBS volume in AWS.

        2. Abstract Storage Management:

        • Applications interact with the PVC rather than directly managing the underlying storage infrastructure.
        • This abstraction simplifies storage management and improves portability.

        3. Specify Storage Requirements:

        • The PVC allows you to define how much storage your application needs (5Gi in this case) and how it will be accessed (ReadWriteOnce).

        4. Enable Stateful Workloads:

        • Many applications, such as databases, require persistent storage to retain data even if the pod is restarted or rescheduled. The PVC ensures that data persists across pod lifecycles.

        Once the PVC is created and bound to a dynamically provisioned EBS volume, we deploy a pod that uses this PVC to verify that everything is working correctly.

        apiVersion: v1kind: Pod  name: ebs-test-pod  containers:      image: busybox # Lightweight image for testing purposes        - sleep # Keep the pod running for testing purposes.      volumeMounts:
                - mountPath: "/data" # Mount point inside the container.
                  name: ebs-volume # Link to volume below.    - name: ebs-volume # Volume linked to PVC.        claimName: ebs-pvc # Reference to PVC created earlier
        kubectl apply -f pod.yaml -n test
        1. Pod-Volume Binding:The pod mounts the dynamically provisioned EBS volume at /data. This verifies that the PVC is correctly bound to a PV and that the volume is accessible from within the pod.
        2. Dynamic Provisioning:Check AWS EC2 > Volumes to confirm that an EBS volume has been automatically created and attached to the worker node where the pod is running.
        3. Data Persistence:Write some data into /data inside the pod, then delete and recreate the pod. Verify that the data is still present, confirming that it persists across pod restarts.

        Testing the Setup: Restarting the Pod to Verify PVC Reattachment

        In this test, we will restart the pod using the existing PVC and verify that it reattaches to the same PersistentVolume (backed by Amazon EBS). This simulates a real-world scenario where a pod is restarted (e.g., due to resource constraints or updates) without deleting and recreating it.

        We will restart the pod (ebs-test-pod) that is already using the PVC (ebs-pvc) to check if it reattaches to the same volume.

        kubectl rollout restart pod ebs-test-pod -n test # check pod is up #Pvc will be nounded to the pod
        • The pod will stop and start again.
        • Kubernetes will reattach the same EBS volume (provisioned by ebs-pvc) to the pod.
        kubectl exec -it ebs-test-pod -- sh

        2. Write Data into testfile.txt:Inside the pod, run:

        echo "Testing EBS Volume Persistence" > /data/testfile.txt

        3. Read and Verify the Data:To check the file:

        Run this command to check if the pod has restarted and is running:

        kubectl get pods
        NAME           READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
        ebs-test-pod   1/1     Running   1          <time>
        • The RESTARTS column should show 1, indicating that the pod has been restarted.
        • The STATUS should be Running.

        3. Verify Data Persistence Inside the Pod

        Access the restarted pod and verify that it can still access data stored on the EBS volume:

        kubectl exec -it ebs-test-pod -- sh
        # Check if previously written data is still available
        cat /data/testfile.txt
        Testing EBS Volume Persistence

        This confirms that data written to the EBS volume before restarting the pod is still accessible.

        What Happens During a Pod Restart?

        • When you restart a pod, Kubernetes stops and starts it on the same node (if possible).
        • During this process, Kubernetes ensures that any PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) used by the pod remains bound to its PersistentVolume (PV).

        2. EBS Volume Reattachment:

        • The Amazon EBS CSI driver ensures that the underlying EBS volume remains attached to the worker node hosting the pod.
        • Since we are not deleting or recreating the PVC, no new volume is provisioned.
        • The data stored on the EBS volume persists across restarts because it is external to the pod’s ephemeral storage.

items.title: NGINX Ingress Controller Setup for Kubernetes Networking
items.author: Ramksai
items.content:
The is a powerful tool for managing external HTTP/HTTPS traffic to Kubernetes workloads. It simplifies routing, enables SSL termination, and provides advanced configurations for handling complex application networking requirements. This guide provides a to setting up NGINX Ingress for routing traffic to multiple applications in an . It also includes and to ensure a .

Why Use NGINX Ingress Controller?

  1. : Supports path-based and host-based routing.
  2. : High throughput with minimal latency.
  3. : Advanced configurations for SSL, rate limiting, and connection handling.
  4. : Extensive documentation and community contributions.
  • : Handles large traffic loads with ease.
  • : Supports custom annotations for advanced use cases.
  • : Works seamlessly across multiple cloud providers.
  • : SSL/TLS termination and security headers.
  • : Requires understanding of networking and Kubernetes concepts.
  • : Managing additional resources (like LoadBalancer) increases operational cost.
  • : Configuration can be challenging for beginners.

SolutionProsConsLightweight, dynamic configuration.Lacks advanced customizations compared to NGINX.High performance, minimal footprint.Steeper learning curve for advanced use cases.Deep integration with AWS services.Limited portability across cloud providers.Advanced traffic management, observability, and security.High complexity and resource overhead.

Why Istio is a Competitor?

Istio is a that provides advanced traffic management, observability, and security for microservices. While it’s not a traditional Ingress Controller, it can be used to manage ingress traffic in Kubernetes.

  1. Advanced Traffic Management:
  • Supports , , and .
  • Provides fine-grained control over traffic routing.
  • Built-in support for , , and .
  • Integrates with tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and Jaeger.
  • Provides for secure communication between services.
  • Supports role-based access control (RBAC)
  • Supports custom for advanced use cases.
  • Requires a deep understanding of .
  • Steeper learning curve compared to NGINX.
  • Adds significant resource overhead due to the .
  • Managing Istio can be more expensive in terms of time and resources.

When to Use Istio Instead of NGINX?

  • If you need advanced traffic management (e.g., canary deployments, traffic splitting).
  • If you require (e.g., metrics, tracing) across your microservices.
  • If you need for secure communication between services.

When to Use NGINX Instead of Istio?

  • If you need a solution for ingress traffic.
  • If you want to avoid the and of a service mesh.
  • If you only need and .

Before starting, ensure the following prerequisites are met:

  • An with worker nodes.
  • helm: Package manager for Kubernetes.
  • AWS CLI: For managing AWS resources.
  • Ensure proper IAM roles for tagging subnets and provisioning resources.
  • Recommended for production environments to handle routing via DNS.
  1. Verify Kubernetes Cluster Access:
Kubernetes control plane is running at https://<cluster-endpoint> CoreDNS is running at https://<cluster-endpoint>/api/v1/namespaces/kube-system/services/kube-dns:dns/proxy

2. Check Existing Subnets and VPC Details:

  1. : Ensure subnets are properly tagged for LoadBalancer usage.
  2. :
kubectl version - client helm version
  1. : Ensure compatibility with the Kubernetes cluster.
  2. Ensure Nodes Have Sufficient Capacity:
  1. : Ensure there’s enough capacity to deploy additional workloads.

Step 1: Install NGINX Ingress Controller

We are installing the NGINX Ingress Controller using Helm, a package manager for Kubernetes. This step ensures that the Ingress Controller is deployed with , , and for production-grade workloads.

  • The default installation may not meet production requirements.
  • Custom configurations ensure the Ingress Controller can handle , , and .
helm repo add ingress-nginx https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx helm repo update
  • : This adds the official NGINX Ingress Helm repository and updates the local Helm repository cache.
  • : Helm repositories are like app stores for Kubernetes applications. Adding the repository allows us to install the NGINX Ingress Controller.

2. Install the NGINX Ingress Controller:

helm install nginx-ingress ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx \ - namespace nginx \ - create-namespace \ - set controller.replicaCount=3 \ - set controller.autoscaling.enabled=true \ - set controller.autoscaling.minReplicas=2 \ - set controller.autoscaling.maxReplicas=5 \ - set controller.config.use-forwarded-headers="true" \ - set controller.config.enable-underscores-in-headers="true"
  • : This installs the NGINX Ingress Controller with:
  • for high availability.
  • to handle traffic spikes.
  • Custom configurations to support and .
  • : These settings ensure the Ingress Controller is , , and .

3. Verify the Installation:

kubectl get pods -n nginx
  • : This lists all the Pods in the nginx namespace.
nginx-ingress-controller-7c6d8f5b5c-2q4v8       1/1     Running   0          2mnginx-ingress-controller-7c6d8f5b5c-5x6w9       1/1     Running   0          2m
nginx-ingress-controller-7c6d8f5b5c-8z7r1       1/1     Running   0          2m
nginx-ingress-admission-create-abcde            0/1     Completed 0          2m
nginx-ingress-admission-patch-xyzab             0/1     Completed 0          2m
  • The Pods with names like nginx-ingress-controller-* are the .
  • The Pods with names like nginx-ingress-admission-* are the Admission Controller Jobs (they run once during installation to set up the webhook).

Run the following command to list all Services in the nginx namespace:

kubectl get svc -n nginx
NAME                                               TYPE           CLUSTER-IP       EXTERNAL-IP                                                                  PORT(S)                      AGE
nginx-ingress-controller                           LoadBalancer   10.100.233.226   k8s-nginx-nginxing-bc31958827-2cf0a2893d0e8457.elb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com   80:31637/TCP,443:30683/TCP   14m
nginx-ingress-controller-admission                 ClusterIP      10.100.211.55

Step 2: Deploy Multi-Tenant Applications

We are deploying two applications (codecrypt and mediahive) in separate namespaces (tenant-a and tenant-b). This simulates a where each application is isolated.

  • : Namespaces provide logical separation between applications, ensuring they don’t interfere with each other.
  • : Multi-tenant setups allow you to run multiple applications on the same cluster without conflicts.
kubectl create namespace tenant-a kubectl create namespace tenant-b
  • : This creates two namespaces, tenant-a and tenant-b.
  • : Namespaces allow you to isolate resources (e.g., Pods, Services) for different applications.

2. Deploy Application 1: CodeCrypt:

  • Create a file named codecrypt.yaml:
apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment  name: codecryptspec:  selector:      app: codecrypt    metadata:        app: codecrypt      containers:        image: nginx:1.21        - containerPort: 80apiVersion: v1metadata:  namespace: tenant-a  selector:  ports:    targetPort: 80
kubectl apply -f codecrypt.yaml

This deploys the codecrypt application with:

  • 2 replicas for high availability.
  • A Service to expose the application internally.
  • : This ensures the application is running and accessible within the cluster.

3. Deploy Application 2: MediaHive:

  • Create a file named mediahive.yaml:
apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment  name: mediahivespec:  selector:      app: mediahive    metadata:        app: mediahive      containers:        image: httpd:2.4        - containerPort: 80apiVersion: v1metadata:  namespace: tenant-b  selector:  ports:    targetPort: 80
kubectl apply -f mediahive.yaml

This deploys the mediahive application with:

  • 2 replicas for high availability.
  • A Service to expose the application internally.

This ensures the application is running and accessible within the cluster.

Step 3: Configure Advanced Ingress Rules

We are creating an to route traffic to the codecrypt and mediahive applications based on the and . We are also enabling and .

  • : Ingress rules define how external traffic is routed to internal services.
  • : SSL/TLS termination ensures secure communication.
  • : Rate limiting protects backend services from abuse.
  1. Create the Ingress Resource:
  • Create a file named ingress.yaml:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1kind: Ingress  name: multi-tenant-ingress  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rate-limit: "100r/s"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rate-limit-burst: "200"  tls:    - codecrypt.example.com    secretName: tls-secret  - host: codecrypt.example.com      paths:        pathType: Prefix          service:            port:  - host: mediahive.example.com      paths:        pathType: Prefix          service:            port:
kubectl apply -f ingress.yaml

This creates an Ingress resource that:

  • Routes traffic to codecrypt for codecrypt.example.com.
  • Routes traffic to mediahive for mediahive.example.com.
  • Enables SSL/TLS termination using the tls-secret.
  • Applies rate limiting to protect the backend services.
  • : This ensures secure and efficient traffic routing.

Step 4: Enable SSL/TLS Termination

We are using to automatically provision and manage SSL/TLS certificates for the Ingress Controller.

  • : SSL/TLS termination ensures that traffic between clients and the Ingress Controller is encrypted.
  • : Cert-manager automates certificate provisioning and renewal.
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/cert-manager/cert-manager/releases/download/v1.11.0/cert-manager.yaml
  • : This installs cert-manager in the cluster.
  • : Cert-manager automates SSL/TLS certificate management.

2. Create a ClusterIssuer for Let’s Encrypt:

  • Create a file named cluster-issuer.yaml:
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1kind: ClusterIssuer  name: letsencrypt-prod  acme:
    server: https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory
    email: admin@example.com      name: letsencrypt-prod    - http01:          class: nginx
kubectl apply -f cluster-issuer.yaml
  • : This creates a ClusterIssuer that uses Let’s Encrypt to issue certificates.
  • : This automates SSL/TLS certificate provisioning for the Ingress Controller.

Step 5: Validate the Setup

We are testing the setup to ensure everything is working as expected.

  • : Ensures that the Ingress Controller is routing traffic correctly, SSL/TLS is working, and rate limiting is applied.
kubectl get ingress -n nginx
NAME                 CLASS    HOSTS                              ADDRESS                                                                  PORTS     AGE
multi-tenant-ingress nginx    codecrypt.example.com,mediahive.example.com   k8s-nginx-nginxing-bc31958827-2cf0a2893d0e8457.elb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com   80, 443   5m
curl -I https://codecrypt.example.comcurl -I https://mediahive.example.com
  • : Both requests should return a 200 OK response.
curl -H "Host: codecrypt.example.com" <EXTERNAL-IP>
  • : If the rate limit is exceeded, the response should be 429 Too Many Requests.

This guide provides a comprehensive walkthrough for setting up the NGINX Ingress Controller in a Kubernetes cluster. By following these steps, you can deploy a ingress solution with , , and . Let me know if you need further assistance!

items.title: Understanding EC2 and EBS: Attach and Mount EBS volume to EC2
items.author: Ramksai
items.content:
Introduction: Why Do We Need EBS?

Elastic Block Store (EBS) is an essential storage solution in AWS that provides high-performance, scalable, and reliable block-level storage for use with EC2 instances. Here are some reasons why EBS is crucial:

  • : Unlike the ephemeral storage provided by instance store volumes, EBS ensures data persistence. Even when an EC2 instance is stopped or terminated, data in the attached EBS volume remains intact.
  • : With multiple volume types, EBS allows fine-tuned configurations for IOPS (Input/Output Operations Per Second) and throughput, catering to a variety of workloads.
  • : EBS volumes can be resized without downtime, ensuring that your storage grows with your application needs.
  • : EBS supports point-in-time snapshots, enabling quick backup and restore processes.

While EBS is a powerful solution, it has some limitations:

  • : EBS volumes are tied to a specific availability zone within a region. They cannot be directly attached across regions.
  • Single Instance Attachment: An EBS volume can only be attached to one EC2 instance at a time (except for Multi-Attach-enabled volumes).
  • : Unlike instance store volumes, EBS incurs additional costs based on storage type, IOPS, and throughput.
  • : High-performance databases requiring low latency and high IOPS.
  • : Applications like Hadoop or Spark needing scalable and high-throughput storage.
  • : Centralized log storage for analytics and monitoring.
  • : Long-term data storage with snapshot capabilities.

Step 1: Setting Up EC2 and EBS

  1. Navigate to the in the AWS Management Console.
  2. Click on and follow these steps:
  3. Choose an Amazon Machine Image (AMI) based on your workload.
  4. Select an instance type (e.g., t2.micro for basic workloads or m5.large for performance-intensive tasks).
  5. Configure instance details such as VPC, subnet, and key pair for SSH access.
  6. Add storage for the root volume (e.g., 8 GiB for general usage).
  7. Configure security group rules to allow SSH (port 22) and any other necessary ports.
  8. Review and launch the instance.
  9. Once launched, note down the and (e.g., us-east-1a).
  1. Navigate to the section under the menu in the AWS Management Console.
  2. Click and configure the following:
  • gp3: General Purpose SSD with customizable IOPS and throughput.
  • io2: Provisioned IOPS SSD for applications needing high IOPS consistency.
  • sc1/st1: Throughput-optimized HDD for large, sequential workloads.

: Specify the volume size (e.g., 20 GiB).

  • For gp3, specify the desired IOPS (up to 16,000) and throughput (up to 1,000 MiB/s).
  • For io2, provision up to 64,000 IOPS.

: Ensure the volume is in the same AZ as your EC2 instance.

(Optional): Create a volume from an existing snapshot if needed.

: Add key-value pairs for organization and billing (e.g., Name=MyEBSVolume, Environment=Production).

Prerequisites Before Attaching EBS

  1. Region and Availability Zone:
  • The EBS volume must be in the as the EC2 instance. For example, if your instance is in us-east-1a, the EBS volume must also be in us-east-1a.

2. Snapshot ID (if applicable):

  • If you’re creating a volume from a snapshot, ensure that the snapshot exists in the same region as the EBS volume.
  • Use tags to manage resources effectively. Tags like Name, Environment, and Project help identify and categorize volumes.

Device Names for EBS Volumes

  1. :
  • When attaching an EBS volume, specify a device name such as /dev/sdb or /dev/sdc. These names represent the logical connection in the instance.
  • Inside the instance, device names may appear as /dev/xvdb, /dev/xvdc, etc., or /dev/nvme0n1 for NVMe-based instances.
  • Use the lsblk command to verify the actual device name in the operating system.
  • Avoid using device names already in use.
  • For consistency, assign device names incrementally (e.g., /dev/sdb, /dev/sdc).

Attaching the EBS Volume to the EC2 Instance

  1. Navigate to the section in the AWS Console.
  2. Select the EBS volume you created.
  3. Click and choose .
  • Select the EC2 instance to attach the volume to.
  • Specify the device name (e.g., /dev/sdb).

SSH into the EC2 instance:

ssh -i <your-key.pem> ec2-user@<instance-public-ip>

Use the lsblk command to confirm the volumes are attached

lsblk
NAME    MAJ:MIN RM  SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINT
xvda    202:0    0   50G  0 disk /
xvdb    202:16   0   20G  0 disk
xvdc    202:32   0   30G  0 disk

Use the lsblk -o +SERIAL command to cross-check the attached volume's VolumeID:

lsblk -o NAME,MAJ:MIN,RM,SIZE,RO,TYPE,MOUNTPOINT,SERIAL
NAME    MAJ:MIN RM  SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINT SERIALxvda    202:0    0   50G  0 disk /         vol-0abcd1234efgh5678
xvdb    202:16   0   20G  0 disk           vol-0ijkl9012mnop3456

The SERIAL column displays the VolumeID of the attached volume, allowing you to cross-verify it with the EBS volume ID in the AWS Console.

Checking Disk Usage Before Mounting

  1. Use the df -h command to display the disk usage:
df -h 
Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/xvda1       50G   10G   40G  20% /

Note that the attached volume does not yet appear in the df -h output since it is not mounted.

Step 2: Preparing and Mounting the EBS Volume

Check if the Disk is Empty

Before formatting the volume, verify that it does not contain any existing data. Run the following command:

Example output for an unformatted disk:

If the disk contains data, the output will indicate the filesystem type (e.g., ext4 or xfs).

If the disk is empty or its data is no longer needed, proceed to format it. You can use either ext4 or xfs as the filesystem type. Here is how to format the volume:

sudo mkfs -t ext4 /dev/xvdb
sudo mkfs -t xfs /dev/xvdb

Check Disk Usage Before Mounting

Before mounting the volume, check the current disk usage using:

df -h
Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/xvda1       50G  1.5G   46G   4% /

Mounting the Disk to a Specific Directory

Once the EBS volume has been formatted, the next step is to mount it to a specific directory. This allows you to use the storage provided by the EBS volume for a particular application or user. In this section, we’ll explore a scenario where we mount the EBS volume to a new user’s home directory under /home/user1.

Scenario: Mounting the Disk to /home/user1

Step 1: Verify the User Directory

1.Ensure the user directory /home/user1 exists on the instance:

2. If the directory does not exist, create it:

sudo mkdir -p /home/user1

3. Set appropriate permissions for the directory:

sudo chown user1:user1 /home/user1

Replace user1 with the actual username.

Step 2: Mount the EBS Volume

  1. Mount the EBS volume to the directory:
sudo mount /dev/xvdb /home/user1

Replace /dev/xvdb with the actual device name of your EBS volume if it differs.

2. Verify the mount using the df -h command:

Example output: Ensure that /home/user1 is listed as a mount point.

Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on /dev/xvda1       50G  1.5G   46G   4% / 
/dev/xvdb        20G   0G    20G   0% /home/user1

Step 3: Test the Mount Point

  1. Create a test file in the /home/user1 directory to confirm it is accessible:
sudo touch /home/user1/testfile.txt

2. Verify the file was created:

ls -l /home/user1/testfile.txt

3. Check disk usage to confirm the file is stored on the mounted volume:

Step 4: Persist the Mount Across Reboots

By default, the mount will not persist across reboots. To make it persistent:

  1. Open the /etc/fstab file for editing:

2. Add an entry for the EBS volume:

/dev/xvdb   /home/user1   ext4   defaults,nofail   0   2

Replace ext4 with the filesystem type you used when formatting (e.g., xfs).

3. Test the fstab configuration:

sudo umount /home/user1 sudo mount -a

Step 5: Unmounting the Disk (Optional)

If you need to unmount the disk, follow these steps:

Ensure /home/user1 is no longer listed as a mount point.

Step 6: Attaching the Disk and Reassign

If you need reuse the same disk for different user. Like if user1 left Organization, we can reuse the disck to user2

First: lets unmount the disk

Second: lets clean the Disk,:

sudo mkfs -t ext4 /dev/xvdb
sudo mkfs -t xfs /dev/xvdb

Once this is cleaned, We can mount to new user2. Imagine the user2 has directory

sudo mount /dev/xvdb /home/user1

Rerun the commands like df -h, lsblk, and Update the fstab with the updated info as this disk is mounted to user2 directory

  • Always ensure no files are in use before unmounting a volume. You can use the lsof command to check:
  • If the disk is no longer needed, consider detaching it from the EC2 instance via the AWS Management Console or CLI.

With these steps, you’ve successfully mounted an EBS volume to a specific user directory, made the mount persistent, and understood how to manage the mount point effectively.

items.title: Kubernetes Backup and Restore: Enhancing Data Protection with Velero
items.author: Ramksai
items.content:
As organizations increasingly adopt cloud-native development practices and Kubernetes, the need for robust data protection strategies becomes paramount. While Kubernetes offers numerous benefits in terms of reliability, scalability, and portability, it also presents unique challenges in data management and protection. Given the highly dynamic nature of Kubernetes environments, where applications and data are constantly changing, frequent and up-to-date backups are essential for disaster recovery, data loss prevention, and mitigation of malicious attacks. However, Kubernetes itself does not provide a built-in backup and restore solution, which creates a significant gap in the data protection strategies of many organizations. This is where Velero steps in.

The Challenge of Kubernetes Backup

Kubernetes environments are inherently complex, and with applications scaling up and down, pods being created and destroyed, and persistent data being frequently updated, ensuring reliable backups becomes a complex task. Traditional backup methods may not be sufficient for cloud-native architectures, as they are often not designed to handle the granular and distributed nature of Kubernetes.

Velero offers a tailored solution to this challenge, specifically designed to backup, restore, and migrate Kubernetes resources and persistent volumes.

is an open-source tool that simplifies Kubernetes cluster backups and restores. It provides a comprehensive approach to safeguarding both configuration data and persistent volumes in Kubernetes clusters. With Velero, organizations can:

  • Backup entire clusters or specific namespaces
  • Restore clusters after disasters or data loss
  • Migrate cluster resources across different environments

Velero’s cloud-agnostic design means it can be deployed on any Kubernetes cluster, whether in the cloud or on-premise, making it an ideal solution for multi-cloud strategies.

Velero stands out with its powerful features:

  • : Velero allows for granular control over the backup process. You can backup an entire cluster, specific namespaces, or even individual resources using label selectors.
  • : Velero supports automated, recurring backups. This ensures that your Kubernetes cluster is continuously protected with up-to-date backups.
  • : Velero allows for the execution of custom scripts before and after backups. This can be used for tasks like pausing applications, flushing caches, or other pre-backup tasks that enhance data integrity.

One of the key differentiators of Velero is its approach to capturing and restoring cluster states. Rather than interacting directly with Kubernetes’ etcd database, Velero interacts with the Kubernetes API to gather resource states. This approach provides several benefits:

  1. Flexible Resource Selection: Velero enables users to selectively backup and restore resources based on their needs, which allows for greater control over data protection and recovery.
  2. Managed Kubernetes Compatibility: Velero works seamlessly with managed Kubernetes services (like AWS EKS, GKE, AKS), where direct access to etcd is often restricted.
  3. : Velero supports resources that are exposed through aggregated API servers, ensuring that custom resources can also be backed up and restored.

In addition to backing up Kubernetes configuration data (like Pods, Services, ConfigMaps, etc.), Velero also supports backing up persistent data using either storage platform snapshots or the file-level backup tool . This ensures that data volumes are protected alongside Kubernetes resources.

Velero integrates directly into Kubernetes through Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs). These CRDs define backup, restore, and scheduling operations that Velero manages using its controllers. Here’s a look at how Velero operates:

  1. : A backup is initiated through the Velero CLI or UI.
  2. : The Kubernetes API server creates a object that defines the backup parameters (e.g., what namespaces, resources, or volumes to include).
  3. BackupController Processing: The BackupController validates and processes the Backup object, ensuring it meets the criteria for the backup job.
  4. : Velero queries the Kubernetes API server to gather the resources specified in the backup request (e.g., Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, PersistentVolumes).
  5. : Once collected, the backup data (both configuration and persistent volumes) is uploaded to the specified object storage service (such as AWS S3, GCS, or Azure Blob Storage).
  1. : A restore is initiated by creating a object through the Velero CLI or UI.
  2. Restore Object Processing: Velero’s RestoreController processes the Restore object, verifying the request and determining which resources to restore.
  3. : Velero re-applies the backed-up Kubernetes resources to the cluster by submitting the backed-up YAML manifests to the API server.
  4. : If persistent volumes were included in the backup, they are reattached to the appropriate pods.

Here are the prerequisites which are required to have velero tested and setup:

  • Ensure you have an active Kubernetes cluster running version 1.28, such as Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) 1.28.
  • The cluster should have sufficient resources (memory, CPU, etc.) to run Velero components and associated workloads.

Note: Check the version compatibility and select the specific version required for velero to be installed in EKS

If Upgrading the velero from v1.9.x to > 1.10.x we must run the following commands to install necessary CRD’s

Caution: From Velero v1.10, except for using restic to do file-system level backup and restore, kopia is also been integrated, so there would be a little bit of difference when upgrading to v1.10 from a version lower than v1.10.0.

Update the Velero custom resource definitions (CRDs) to include schema changes across all CRDs that are at the core of the new features in this release:

velero install --crds-only --dry-run -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

NOTE: Since velero v1.10.0 only v1 CRD will be supported during installation, therefore, the v1.10.0 will only work on kubernetes version >= v1.16

  • The (Command Line Interface) must be installed and configured on your local machine or the system where you are setting up Velero.
  • Configure AWS CLI with sufficient IAM permissions to manage the S3 bucket, EBS volumes, and any other AWS resources used by Velero.
  • Velero requires an IAM role or user with the following minimum permissions to back up and restore resources:
  • : For storing and retrieving backup files.
  • : To create snapshots of persistent volumes backed by Amazon EBS.
  • The IAM role should have these policies attached: AmazonS3FullAccess, AmazonEC2FullAccess, AmazonEKSClusterPolicy.
  • Create an S3 bucket where Velero will store backup files. The bucket should be in the same AWS region as your EKS cluster.
  • Ensure that the S3 bucket has the proper permissions and lifecycle policies, if required, for data retention.
aws s3 mb s3://<your-velero-backups-bucket> --region <your-region>
  • Install the on your local machine, which is required to interact with the Velero server on your Kubernetes cluster.
# MacOSbrew install velerocurl -L https://github.com/vmware-tanzu/velero/releases/download/v1.11.0/velero-v1.11.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz | tar -xz
sudo mv velero-v1.11.0-linux-amd64/velero /usr/local/bin/

6. Storage Class for Snapshots

To ensure Velero operates smoothly within your EKS cluster, you’ll need to configure certain AWS services, such as IAM roles and OIDC providers. Here’s a step-by-step guide for setting up the required IAM components and CSI driver for Amazon EBS.

6.1 — Step 1: Create an IAM OIDC Provider for Your Cluster

Velero requires IAM roles to perform operations like accessing S3 for backups and managing persistent volumes. You can configure an IAM OIDC provider for your EKS cluster as follows:

  1. Identify OIDC ID for the Cluster:
oidc_id=$(aws eks describe-cluster --name <Your-Cluster-Name> --query "cluster.identity.oidc.issuer" --output text | cut -d '/' -f 5)

2. Check for Existing OIDC Providers:

aws iam list-open-id-connect-providers | grep $oidc_id

3. Associate OIDC Provider with Your Cluster:

eksctl utils associate-iam-oidc-provider --region=<Your-Region> --cluster=<Your-Cluster-Name> --approve

4. Verify OIDC Provider Association:

aws iam list-open-id-connect-providers | grep $oidc_id
Arn: arn:aws:iam::<Account-ID>:oidc-provider/oidc.eks.<Your-Region>.amazonaws.com/id/<Your-OIDC-ID>

6.2 — Step 2: Create Amazon EBS CSI Driver IAM Role

Velero uses Amazon EBS CSI drivers to manage persistent volumes in EKS. You’ll need to create an IAM role for the EBS CSI driver and attach it to your EKS cluster.

  1. Create the EBS CSI IAM Role Using 
eksctl create iamserviceaccount \  --name ebs-csi-controller-sa \
  --namespace kube-system \
  --cluster <Your-Cluster-Name> \
  --attach-policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicy \
  --approve \  --role-name AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole
#make sure to change the eks cluster--cluster <Your-Cluster-Name>

This setup ensures that the EKS cluster is correctly configured to support Velero for backups and persistent volume snapshots.

UI : To setup, Go to EKS cluster in AWS. Go to ADD-ONs and add this EBS CSI Driver

  • Create a namespace where Velero will operate, typically velero.
kubectl create namespace velero

8. Network and Security Groups

  • Verify that the network and security groups associated with your EKS cluster allow for necessary ingress and egress traffic. Velero requires network connectivity to AWS services, including the S3 bucket and EC2 for snapshot creation.
  • Configure proper inbound and outbound rules for Velero pods, especially if they run in a VPC with restrictive policies.

To install NGINX on your EKS 1.28 cluster as a demo for Velero, follow these simple steps:

  • Ensure kubectl is configured to interact with your EKS cluster:
aws eks --region us-west-1 update-kubeconfig --name eks1
kubectl config current-context
#will print the current k8s cluster
  • Create a namespace for the NGINX deployment:
kubectl create namespace nginx-demo
  • Create a NGINX deployment:
kubectl create deployment nginx --image=nginx -n nginxNAME                         READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
pod/nginx-7854ff8877-z7n5w   1/1     Running   0          18s
NAME                    READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
deployment.apps/nginx   1/1     1            1           18s
NAME                               DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   AGE
replicaset.apps/nginx-7854ff8877   1         1         1       18s

These are some of the prerequisites for configuring the Velero in EKS.

Velero Backup Restore (EKS1, EKS2)

Primary EKS1 Cluster Setup:

If you are using cluster config and to access cluster you need IAM User or Role. We might already use creds to access the cluster. So if you want to use same user for Velero we need a policy for backup and restore into S3 bucket. This policy must be attached to IAM user.

If you want to separate the IAM Users. If a new user is required only for this Disaster Management we can create a separate users. Here in this doc we are separating the Users.

Step 1: Create an S3 Bucket

  1. Log in to the AWS Management Console.
  2. and click .
  3. Configure the bucket settings:
  • : velero-eks1-bucket (or any globally unique name).
  • : us-east-1 (or your preferred AWS region).
  • : Block all public access for security.
  • : Enable versioning (recommended for Velero).

S3 Access. Run the following command to verify that your credentials and permissions are correctly set up for S3 access:

This should list the contents of the bucket, or return an empty result if the bucket is empty. If there’s an access error, review the IAM policy attached to the user or role.

aws s3 ls s3://velero-eks-globala --region us-east-1

Step 2: Create an IAM User for Velero

  1. in the AWS Console and create a new user:
  • : Programmatic access (required for CLI and API).

to the user: [Need to update s3 bucket]

  • : This policy will allow Velero to manage backups in the S3 bucket.
{    "Version": "2012-10-17",        {            "Action": [                "ec2:DescribeSnapshots",                "ec2:CreateVolume",                "ec2:DeleteSnapshot"            "Resource": "*"        {            "Action": [                "s3:DeleteObject",                "s3:AbortMultipartUpload",
                "s3:ListMultipartUploadParts"
            ],                "arn:aws:s3:::velero-eks-cluster-backup/*"        },            "Effect": "Allow",                "s3:ListBucket"            "Resource": [
                "arn:aws:s3:::velero-eks-cluster-backup"
            ]    ]EOF

3. Save AccessKey/SecretKey in a file. Steps to Save AWS Credentials in a Different File and Pass Them to Velero:

3.1 Create a Separate Credentials File:

  • Save your AWS credentials in a file at a custom path. For example, create a file named velero-creds in your home directory:
  • :
[default]aws_access_key_id = YOUR_ACCESS_KEY_ID
aws_secret_access_key = YOUR_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY

Step 3: Create IAM ServiceAccount EKS1

Primary Cluster Name: EKS1

The allows your EKS cluster to manage (Elastic Block Store) for your applications running on Kubernetes. It provides dynamic provisioning of persistent storage for your workloads.

  • Without this IAM ServiceAccount and associated IAM role, the EBS CSI driver wouldn’t have the necessary permissions to manage EBS volumes in AWS (e.g., creating and attaching volumes).

1: Create the EBS CSI IAM Role Using

This command sets up the that allow the Amazon EBS CSI (Container Storage Interface) Driver to interact with AWS services (like EBS volumes) on behalf of your EKS cluster.

eksctl create iamserviceaccount \  --name ebs-csi-controller-sa \
  --namespace kube-system \
  --cluster eks1 \
  --attach-policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicy \
  --approve \  --role-name AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole
#make sure to change the eks cluster--cluster <Your-Cluster-Name>

This setup ensures that the EKS cluster is correctly configured to support Velero for backups and persistent volume snapshots.

To setup, Go to EKS cluster in AWS. Go to ADD-ONs and add this EBS CSI Driver

Check the ServiceAccount in Kube-System

kubectl get sa ebs-csi-controller-sa -n kube-system -o yamlapiVersion: v1
automountServiceAccountToken: true
kind: ServiceAccount  annotations:
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxxxxx:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole_eks1
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/component: csi-driver
    app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: EKS
    app.kubernetes.io/name: aws-ebs-csi-driver
  name: ebs-csi-controller-sa

2: Install Velero in EKS — Primary:

Update the Velero custom resource definitions (CRDs) to include schema changes across all CRDs that are at the core of the new features in this release:

velero install --crds-only --dry-run -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

NOTE: Since velero v1.10.0 only v1 CRD will be supported during installation, therefore, the v1.10.0 will only work on kubernetes version >= v1.16

  • Velero IAM user, we have created IAM Policy attached to this user and we have AWS credentials in a file at a custom path.
  • :
[default]aws_access_key_id = YOUR_ACCESS_KEY_ID
aws_secret_access_key = YOUR_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY

Required Parameters: Velero Version, AWS plugin, Credential FilePath, Bucket

velero install \  --provider aws \
  --image velero/velero:v1.14.0 \
  --plugins velero/velero-plugin-for-aws:v1.10.0 \
  --bucket velero-eks-cluster-backup \
  --secret-file ~/velero-creds \
  --backup-location-config region=us-east-1 \
  --snapshot-location-config region=us-east-1 \
kubectl get all -n veleroNAME                          READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
pod/velero-xxxxx-xxxx         1/1     Running   0          26d
NAME                     READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE    AGE
deployment.apps/velero    1/1     1            1           26d

First, ensure that the nginx namespace exists. If not, create it:

kubectl create namespace nginx

Now, deploy the NGINX app in the nginx namespace. You can do this using a simple deployment with a service for accessing NGINX:

kind: Deployment  name: nginx-deploymentspec:  selector:      app: nginx    metadata:        app: nginx      containers:        image: nginx:latest        - containerPort: 80

Once the deployment and service are running correctly:

kubectl get pods -n nginx

We can check the current NS

NAME              STATUS   AGE
default           Active   26d
kube-node-lease   Active   26d
kube-public       Active   26d
kube-system       Active   26d
test              Active   25d
velero            Active   26d
nginx             Active   26d

Velero uses S3 to store EKS backups when running in AWS. With Velero installed, we will move forward with installing an application in our Primary cluster that we will back up and restore in our Recovery cluster. Customers will be able to follow the steps below to back up and restore their own applications in their own Amazon EKS clusters as well.

Create a backup of the Primary cluster. Be sure to switch your kubectl context back to the Primary cluster before running the command below.

 : When we execute velero backup command, it will make a call to the Kubernetes API server and create a backup of objects like namespaces, storageclass, pv, pvc etc. The backup controller will query to the API server for the objects to be backed up and then makes a call to the S3 bucket where the backup will be saved in tar format.

To create periodic backups with Velero by configuring a ‘schedule’.

This is to describe the backup and status of the backup

Here are the commands to take backup of single ns, backup excluding few ns, wholecluster backup

— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —

Here are the backup commands can be used for velero

# Create a backup every 6 hours with the @every notation
velero schedule create <SCHEDULE_NAME> --schedule="@every 6h"
# Create a daily backup of the namespace
velero schedule create <SCHEDULE_NAME> --schedule="@every 24h" --include-namespaces <namspacename>
# Create a weekly backup, each living for 90 days (2160 hours)
velero schedule create <SCHEDULE_NAME> --schedule="@every 168h" --ttl 2160h0m0s    
##default TTL time is 720h
# Create a backup including the test and default namespaces
velero backup create backup --include-namespaces test,default
# Create a backup excluding the kube-system and default namespaces
velero backup create backup --exclude-namespaces kube-system,default
# To backup entire cluster
velero backup create <BACKUPNAME>
#To backup namespace in a cluster
velero backup create <BACKUPNAME> --include-namespaces <NAMESPACENAME>
# Daily Backup, 7 days retention policy
velero create schedule wordpress-bkup-daily-ret-7d \--ttl 168h0m0s \
--include-namespaces wordpress-ns
# Daily Backup, 7 days retention policy
velero schedule create duokey-bkup-daily-ret-7d --schedule="0 0 * * *" --ttl 168h0m0s --selector app=duokey
# Weekly Backup, 30 days retention policy
velero schedule create duokey-bkup-weekly-ret-30d --schedule="1 0 */7 * *" --ttl 720h0m0s --selector app=duokey
# Monthly Backup, 12 months retention policy
velero schedule create duokey-bkup-monthly-ret-12m --schedule="0 8 1 * *" --ttl 8760h0m0s --selector app=duokey
# Yearly Backup, 3 years retention policy
velero schedule create duokey-bkup-yearly-ret-3y --schedule="0 16 1 5 *" --ttl 43800h0m0s --selector app=duokey

Create Backup — Single NS

Here is the commands I used to create a schedule for the backup. I want to schedule the backup during off hours. The backup which I schedule must be deleted after 30 min and take backup every 30 min.

1: Create the EBS CSI IAM Role Using eksctl or UI:

This command sets up the that allow the Amazon EBS CSI (Container Storage Interface) Driver to interact with AWS services (like EBS volumes) on behalf of your EKS cluster.

eksctl create iamserviceaccount \  --name ebs-csi-controller-sa \

To setup, Go to EKS cluster in AWS. Go to ADD-ONs and add this EBS CSI Driver

2: Install Velero in EKS — Secondary:
