  • Velero IAM user, we have created IAM Policy attached to this user and we have AWS credentials in a file at a custom path.
  • :
[default]aws_access_key_id = YOUR_ACCESS_KEY_ID
aws_secret_access_key = YOUR_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY

3. Velero Install Scondary

Required Parameters: Velero Version, AWS plugin, Credential FilePath, Bucket

velero install \  --provider aws \
  --image velero/velero:v1.14.0 \
  --plugins velero/velero-plugin-for-aws:v1.10.0 \
  --bucket velero-eks-cluster-backup \
  --secret-file ~/velero-creds \
  --backup-location-config region=us-east-1 \
  --snapshot-location-config region=us-east-1 \
kubectl get all -n veleroNAME                          READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
pod/velero-xxxxx-xxxx         1/1     Running   0          26d
NAME                     READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE    AGE
deployment.apps/velero    1/1     1            1           26d

We can use use the velero restore createcommand is used to restore resources and data that have been backed up using Velero. Here’s a detailed description of how the restore process works:

Velero Restore Commands :

 create a restore named "restore-1" from backup "backup-1"velero restore create restore-1 --from-backup backup-1
# create a restore with a default name ("backup-1-<timestamp>") from backup "backup-1"
velero restore create --from-backup backup-1
# create a restore from the latest successful backup triggered by schedule "schedule-1"
velero restore create --from-schedule schedule-1
# create a restore from the latest successful OR partially-failed backup triggered by schedule "schedule-1"
velero restore create --from-schedule schedule-1 --allow-partially-failed
# create a restore for only persistentvolumeclaims and persistentvolumes within a backup
velero restore create --from-backup backup-2 --include-resources persistentvolumeclaims,persistentvolumesvelero restore create --from-backup <backupname>velero restore create <RESTORE_NAME> --from-schedule <SCHEDULE_NAME>
# Create a restore including the test and default namespaces
velero restore create --from-backup backup --include-namespaces nginx,default
# Create a restore excluding the kube-system and default namespaces
velero restore create --from-backup backup --exclude-namespaces kube-system,defaultvelero restore logs <RESTORE_NAME>

How Velero Restore Works:

  • Velero retrieves the backup data stored in your backup storage location (e.g., S3 for AWS).
  • It then restores Kubernetes resources, such as Pods, Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets, etc., to the specified namespace or the original namespace if not specified.

Restoring Persistent Volumes:

  • If the backup includes persistent volume claims (PVCs), Velero restores them along with the associated volumes (e.g., EBS volumes in AWS).
  • Velero may need to recreate volumes and restore data from snapshots if configured.

Restoring Configuration and Secrets:

  • Configuration data, secrets, and other Kubernetes objects are restored as they were at the time of the backup.

Handling Resource Conflicts:

  • If resources already exist in the cluster with the same name as those being restored, Velero may overwrite them or handle conflicts based on the restore options specified.
  • After initiating the restore, you can check the status of the restore operation to see if it completed successfully or if there were any errors.

— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —

To restore backups from eks1 to eks2, you need to follow a series of steps to ensure that the resources from eks1 are correctly restored into the eks2 cluster. Here’s a step-by-step guide to document the restore process:

  • : Ensure that Velero is installed and configured in the eks2 cluster.
  • : The backups from eks1 (e.g., test1 and wholecluster) should be available in the backup storage location (e.g., S3 bucket).

Steps to Restore Backups in eks2:

  • Ensure that Velero can see the backups from eks1 in the backup storage location.

items.title: Helm3 Basic Understanding
items.author: Ramksai
items.content:
Helm is a popular package manager for Kubernetes, designed to simplify the deployment and management of applications on Kubernetes clusters. This documentation aims to provide you with a comprehensive guide to using Helm 3 effectively.

Helm is a package manager for Kubernetes, the open-source platform used to manage containerized applications. It is often compared to traditional package managers like apt for Debian-based systems or yum for RPM-based systems, but it is specifically designed for Kubernetes.

Helm helps you manage Kubernetes applications through Helm charts. A Helm chart is a collection of files that describe a related set of Kubernetes resources. It simplifies the process of defining, installing, and upgrading even the most complex Kubernetes applications.

Using Helm brings several benefits, particularly in the context of managing Kubernetes applications:

Using Helm brings several benefits, particularly in the context of managing Kubernetes applications:

  1. Simplified Application Deployment:
  • Helm charts allow you to define, install, and upgrade Kubernetes applications quickly and easily.
  • With a single Helm command, you can deploy a multi-component application with all its dependencies.
  • Helm allows you to version your Kubernetes applications, making it easy to roll back to a previous version if needed.
  • Each release of a chart is tracked and versioned, ensuring you can manage upgrades and downgrades smoothly.

3. Reuse and Share Charts:

  • Helm charts can be shared and reused, promoting collaboration within and between teams.
  • You can find pre-built charts for popular applications in public repositories like Helm Hub.

4. Configuration Management:

  • Helm provides a way to manage and customize your application’s configuration using values files and template files.
  • This allows you to manage complex configurations and use the same chart for multiple environments (development, staging, production) with different configurations.

:

  • Helm can manage dependencies for your charts. If your application relies on other applications or services, Helm can ensure all dependencies are installed and configured correctly.

Helm vs. Traditional Kubernetes YAML

Helm offers several advantages over managing Kubernetes applications using traditional YAML files:

  1. Modularity and Reusability:
  • Helm charts promote modularity by allowing you to break down your Kubernetes configurations into smaller, reusable components.
  • Traditional YAML files tend to be monolithic and specific to a particular environment.

2. Easier Upgrades and Rollbacks:

  • Helm makes it straightforward to upgrade applications and roll back to previous versions, handling all the complexities of Kubernetes resource management.
  • With traditional YAML files, manual intervention is often required to update or revert changes.

3. Consistent and Repeatable Deployments:

  • Helm charts ensure that deployments are consistent and repeatable across different environments.
  • Traditional YAML deployments might vary between environments, leading to potential inconsistencies.

4. Parameterization and Templates:

  • Helm charts support parameterization and templating, enabling dynamic configuration based on the deployment environment.
  • Traditional YAML files lack built-in templating and often require manual edits for different configurations.

:

  • Helm can manage dependencies between charts, ensuring that all required components are installed and configured.
  • Managing dependencies with traditional YAML files can be cumbersome and error-prone.

Helm 3 simplified the architecture by removing Tiller, leading to a more secure and streamlined process. Now, the Helm Client directly interacts with the Kubernetes API server.

  • : The Helm Client remains a command-line tool for managing charts. It directly communicates with the Kubernetes API server to perform operations such as installations, upgrades, and rollbacks.
  • : With Helm 3, the Helm Client interacts directly with the Kubernetes API server, leveraging native Kubernetes RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) for security and access control.

Helm consists of several key components:

  1. : A Helm chart is a collection of files that describe a Kubernetes application. Charts are packaged as .tgz files and can be shared via repositories.
  2. : Helm repositories are collections of packaged charts. They provide a way to distribute charts and make them available for installation.
  3. : A release is an instance of a chart running in a Kubernetes cluster. Helm manages the lifecycle of these releases, including installation, upgrade, and rollback.
  4. : Templates are files in a chart that contain Kubernetes resource definitions written in YAML, but with placeholders for dynamic values. These placeholders are replaced with actual values when the chart is deployed.
  1. Helm Search: Finding Charts
  • Use helm search to discover available charts in configured repositories. For example:

2. Repo Add: Adding Helm Repositories

  • Add new Helm repositories to access charts from different sources. For instance:
helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami

3. Pull Helm: Downloading Charts

  • Download Helm charts without installing them using helm pull. Example:

4. Repo Update: Updating Repositories

  • Update the local cache of Helm repositories to get the latest charts and metadata:

5. Show Chart: Viewing Chart Details

  • Display detailed information about a specific Helm chart, such as its versions and dependencies:
helm show chart bitnami/nginx

6. Show Values: Viewing Chart Values

  • View the default configuration values used by a Helm chart with helm show values:
helm show values bitnami/nginx

7. Install: Deploying a Helm Chart

  • Deploy a Helm chart onto your Kubernetes cluster using helm install.
helm install my-nginx bitnami/nginx
  • Deploy a Helm chart onto your Kubernetes cluster using helm install. You can pass arguments directly during installation using the --set flag to override specific values from the chart's values.yaml.

Let’s say you want to install the NGINX chart (bitnami/nginx) and customize some values during installation.

helm install my-nginx bitnami/nginx --set replicaCount=3,image.tag=latest -n nginx
  • my-nginx is the name you give to the Helm release.
  • bitnami/nginx specifies the chart repository name and chart name.
  • --set is used to override specific values defined in the chart's values.yaml.
  • replicaCount=3 sets the number of replicas to 3.
  • image.tag=latest sets the Docker image tag to latest.

7.1 Check Helm Release Status

After installing, you can use the following commands to check the status and details of your Helm release:

: This command lists all installed Helm releases on the Kubernetes cluster.

Show Detailed Information about a Release: This command shows detailed information about the my-nginx release, including configuration, status, and Kubernetes resources.

helm get all my-nginx -n nginx

This Kubernetes command retrieves the status of pods belonging to the my-nginx release, filtering by the label app.kubernetes.io/instance=my-nginx.

8. Generate Helm Manifest YAML

After installing a Helm release using helm install, you can use the helm manifest command to generate Kubernetes YAML manifests for that release. Here’s how you can do it:

helm manifest my-nginx -n nginx

This command renders the templates (templates/) used in the NGINX chart, including Deployment, Service, and other resources, based on the values provided during installation (values.yaml, or overridden with --set flags).

The output of helm manifest will be the combined Kubernetes YAML manifests for all resources managed by the my-nginx release, including labels, annotations, and other metadata defined in the Helm chart.

Redirecting Output to a File: If you want to save the generated YAML to a file, you can redirect the output using standard shell redirection. This will save the generated Kubernetes manifests into a file named my-nginx-manifest.yaml in your current directory.

helm manifest my-nginx > my-nginx-manifest.yaml 
cat my-nginx-manifest.yaml

9. Upgrade the Helm Release

  • This command upgrades the existing my-nginx release deployed from the bitnami/nginx chart, changing the replicaCount from 3 to 5.
helm upgrade my-nginx bitnami/nginx --set replicaCount=5 -n nginx

10. Uninstall: Removing a Helm Release

  • Remove a Helm release and its associated resources from your Kubernetes cluster with helm uninstall:
helm uninstall my-nginx -n nginx

To create a Helm chart, you can use the helm create <chart-name> command. This command generates a directory structure with several predefined files and directories. Let's walk through this command and explain the structure it creates:

This command will create a directory named mychart with the following structure:

# Chart Structure and Files

nginx/  ├── charts/  │   ├── deployment.yaml  ├── Chart.yaml

defines metadata about the Helm chart (mychart), including dependencies (

contains configurable values for mychart, including settings for NGINX deployment and MySQL configuration.

templates/deployment.yaml is a Kubernetes Deployment template for NGINX, using values from values.yaml to customize the deployment and integrate with dependencies like MySQL.

apiVersion: v2name: mychart
description: A Helm chart for Kubernetes
type: applicationappVersion: 1.0.0  - name: mysql    repository: https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami
  • : Specifies the API version of the Helm chart (v2 in this case).
  • : The unique name of the Helm chart (mychart in this example).
  • : A brief description of what the Helm chart does.
  • : Indicates that this chart is an application chart.
  • : The version of the Helm chart itself (0.1.0).
  • : The version of the application being deployed by the chart (1.0.0).
  • : Lists other charts (mysql in this case) that this chart depends on. Dependencies ensure that required services are installed along with the main application.

The dependencies section specifies that mychart depends on the MySQL chart (bitnami/mysql) version 1.6.7 from the Bitnami repository. This means when you install or upgrade mychart, Helm will also manage the lifecycle of mysql chart, ensuring it is installed, upgraded, or removed as necessary.

How Dependencies Are Used in Deployment

name: my-nginxnamespace: default  repository: nginx  port: 80  mysqlRootPassword: rootpassword  mysqlPassword: mypassword
  mysqlDatabase: mydatabase
  • : Specifies the name of the NGINX deployment (my-nginx).
  • : The Kubernetes namespace where the deployment will be installed (default in this case).
  • : Defines the Docker image details for NGINX (nginx:1.19.2).
  • : Specifies details for the Kubernetes Service associated with NGINX.
  • : Refers to the name of a Kubernetes Secret (my-secret) containing sensitive data.
  • : Contains configuration values specific to MySQL, such as passwords and database names.

templates/deployment.yaml

apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment  name: {{ .Values.name }}-nginx
  namespace: {{ .Values.namespace }}
  labels:spec:  selector:      app: {{ .Values.name }}    metadata:        app: {{ .Values.name }}      containers:
        - name: {{ .Values.name }}-nginx
          image: "{{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag }}"            - containerPort: {{ .Values.service.port }}            - name: MYSQL_HOST
              value: {{ .Release.Name }}-mysql
            - name: MYSQL_DATABASE
              value: {{ .Values.mysql.mysqlDatabase }}
            - name: MYSQL_USER
              value: {{ .Values.mysql.mysqlUser }}
            - name: MYSQL_PASSWORD                secretKeyRef:
                  name: {{ .Values.secretName }}
                  key: mysql-password
  • This deployment.yaml template defines a Kubernetes Deployment for NGINX.
  • It uses values from values.yaml (like .Values.name, .Values.namespace, .Values.image.repository, etc.) to configure the deployment.
  • Environment variables (MYSQL_HOST, MYSQL_DATABASE, MYSQL_USER, MYSQL_PASSWORD) are set using values from values.yaml and the Helm release context (Release.Name).
  • MYSQL_HOST is set to the hostname of the MySQL service provided by the dependency ({{ .Release.Name }}-mysql).

Installing NGINX using Helm

Now, let’s proceed with deploying NGINX using Helm:

Initialize Helm (if not already initialized):

Navigate to the directory containing your Helm chart (:

  • my-nginx is the release name you assign.
  • . indicates that the current directory (nginx/) contains the Helm chart to be installed.
helm install my-nginx . -n nginx

:

helm uninstall my-nginx -n nginx

items.title: Configuring Grafana with AWS EBS Persistent Storage and Custom Dashboards, Plugins and DataSources…
items.author: Ramksai
items.content:
Configuring Grafana with AWS EBS Persistent Storage and Custom Dashboards, Plugins and DataSources on Amazon EKS

In this guide, we will configure Grafana on Amazon EKS using Persistent Volume Claims (PVC) and Storage Classes to ensure data persistence, even if the pod restarts. We’ll also create custom dashboards for monitoring.

Setting Up Grafana with PVC and Storage Class

Grafana is a powerful tool for monitoring and observability. By using PVCs and Storage Classes, we can store Grafana data on Amazon EBS (Elastic Block Store), ensuring that our data remains intact across pod restarts.

Persistent storage is essential for applications that require data retention, such as Grafana. Amazon EBS provides reliable and high-performance block storage that integrates seamlessly with EKS.

Creating Custom Dashboards

Grafana allows you to create custom dashboards tailored to your specific monitoring needs. We’ll guide you through setting up these dashboards, enabling you to visualize and analyze your metrics effectively.

This document assumes you have a basic understanding of Kubernetes, EKS, and Helm. If you’re new to any of these concepts, it’s recommended to review my respective documentation before proceeding.

Let’s get started with setting up Grafana on EKS with persistent storage and custom dashboards.

  1. EKS cluster IAM OIDC — Enable
  2. IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA)
  3. Enable EBS CSI Driver as Addon

Enabling IAM OIDC Provider for EKS Cluster

Enable IAM OIDC using Eksctl:

To enable IAM OIDC provider for your EKS cluster, run the following command using CLI

eksctl utils associate-iam-oidc-provider --region <your-region> --cluster <your-cluster-name> --approve

Replace <your-region> and <your-cluster-name> with your AWS region and EKS cluster name respectively.

Another approach, If eksctl is difficult to use try following the below steps in UI

Enable IAM OIDC using AWS UI:

  • Under the “Configuration” tab, scroll down to the “Details” section.
  • Click on the “Enable IAM OIDC provider” button.

Enabling the EBS CSI Driver Add-on via AWS Management Console

  1. Choose the EKS cluster where you want to enable the EBS CSI driver.
  2. Click on the “Add-ons” tab within your cluster’s details page.
  3. Install the EBS CSI Driver:Click “Add add-on”.Select “Amazon EBS CSI Driver” from the list of available add-ons.

    Choose the version you want to install and click “Install”.

After installing the EBS CSI driver using the console or eksctl, verify that the driver is running correctly:

kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=ebs-csi-controller

You should see a list of running pods for the EBS CSI driver controller. With the EBS CSI driver installed and running, you’re ready to configure the StorageClass and PVC for your Grafana deployment.

Creating IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA)

Check the Role if its already existed for the cluster, if not create one:

Create IAM Role with Required Policy:

Create a JSON file named trust-policy.json with the following content. This policy allows the EKS service account to assume the IAM role:

{  "Version": "2012-10-17",    {      "Principal": {
        "Federated": "arn:aws:iam::<your-account-id>:oidc-provider/oidc.eks.<your-region>.amazonaws.com/id/<eks-cluster-id>"
      },
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity",
      "Condition": {          "oidc.eks.<your-region>.amazonaws.com/id/<eks-cluster-id>:sub": "system:serviceaccount:kube-system:ebs-csi-controller-sa"      }  ]

<eks-cluster-id> with your AWS account ID, region, and EKS cluster ID respectively.

Create the IAM role with the trust policy:

aws iam create-role --role-name AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole --assume-role-policy-document file://trust-policy.json

Create the Service Account in Kubernetes:

Change the Account number where IAM role is created

eksctl create iamserviceaccount \  --name ebs-csi-controller-sa \
  --namespace kube-system \
  --cluster <your-cluster-name> \
  --attach-role-arn arn:aws:iam::<your-account-id>:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole \
  --approve \

Copy the below yaml and run the following command to create the SA

kubectl apply -f ebs-csi-controller-sa.yaml -n kube-system
#ebs-csi-controller-sa.yaml automountServiceAccountToken: truemetadata:    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::<your-account-id>:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole    app.kubernetes.io/component: csi-driver
    app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: EKS
    app.kubernetes.io/name: aws-ebs-csi-driver
  name: ebs-csi-controller-sa
  namespace: kube-system

Clone the Code Using Feature Branch

Clone the Grafana Helm chart repository using the feature branch:

git clone https://github.com/Ramksai07/grafana-automation.git

The grafana ConfigMap is used to configure various aspects of Grafana within your Kubernetes environment. It includes settings for plugins, Grafana configuration, datasources, and dashboard providers.

grafana-helm-chart-develop/templates/configmap.yaml

Specifies the Grafana plugins to be installed and used:

plugins: |  grafana-timestream-datasource,alexanderzobnin-zabbix-app,ClickHouse,volkovlabs-echarts-panel

Grafana Configuration (grafana.ini)

Configures various settings for Grafana:

#make sure to use the right domain, root_url this will be configured in R53,

grafana.ini: |  [analytics]  [grafana_net]
  url = https://grafana.net
  [log]  [paths]  logs = /var/log/grafana
  plugins = /var/lib/grafana/plugins
  provisioning = /etc/grafana/provisioning  domain = 'grafana-test.xxx.net'
  root_url= 'https://grafana-test.xxx.net'

Datasources Configuration (datasources.yaml)

Defines data sources for Grafana: Change the sources

 datasources.yaml: |    apiVersion: 1    - name: CloudWatch      jsonData:        defaultRegion: us-east-1

Dashboard Providers (dashboardproviders.yaml)

Specifies providers for custom dashboards:

dashboardproviders.yaml: |  apiVersion: 1    - name: 'custom-dashboards'      folder: ''      disableDeletion: false        path: /var/lib/grafana/dashboards
        foldersFromFilesStructure: true
  1. : Apply the ConfigMap to your Kubernetes cluster using kubectl apply -f grafana-configmap.yaml.
  2. Integration with Helm Charts: If using Helm, integrate this ConfigMap into your Helm deployment for Grafana to ensure consistent configuration across deployments.
  3. : Modify the respective YAML sections (plugins, grafana.ini, datasources.yaml, dashboardproviders.yaml) to reflect any changes needed in Grafana plugins, configuration settings, datasources, or dashboard providers. Apply changes using kubectl apply -f grafana-configmap.yaml.

This structured approach separates the configurations for plugins, datasources, and dashboard providers, making it easier for readers to understand and update specific sections according to their requirements. Adjust paths and settings as per your environment and Grafana setup needs.

Create a Namespace in Kubernetes

Create a namespace for your Grafana deployment:

kubectl create namespace grafana-test

Build and Apply Helm Template (Optional)

If needed, build the Helm template from your cloned code and apply it to Kubernetes. Otherwise, you can directly install the Helm chart.

helm template grafana-helm-chart grafana-helm-chart -n grafana-test > grafana-test.yaml

Verify the generated template:

kubectl apply -f grafana-test.yaml -n grafana-test

To Install Helm Chart Directly:

helm install grafana-helm-chart grafana-helm-chart -n grafana-test

Post Deployment Verification

After deploying Grafana, verify the resources and set up DNS for access.

kubectl get pods -n grafana-testkubectl get pv -n grafana-test
kubectl get pvc -n grafana-test
kubectl get sc -n grafana-test
kubectl get svc -n grafana-test

Grab Load Balancer IP for DNS Setup

Find the External IP or Load Balancer endpoint:

kubectl get svc -n grafana-testNAME      TYPE           CLUSTER-IP      EXTERNAL-IP                       PORT(S)                      AGE
grafana   LoadBalancer   xx.xx.xx.xx   kxxxxxxxxx.elb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com   80:31139/TCP,443:32747/TCP   75m

Configure DNS in AWS Route 53

Create a CNAME record pointing to the External IP or Load Balancer endpoint obtained above in AWS Route 53 for DNS resolution.

Note: Remember to change the domainname in configmap.yaml in the repo

domain = 'grafana-test.xxx.net'root_url= 'https://grafana-test.xxx.net'

These steps outline the process from cloning your Helm chart repository, deploying Grafana using Helm, to setting up DNS for external access. Let me know if you need further customization or additional steps!

items.title: Restrict Pod Permissions To Access AWS Resources
items.author: Ramksai
items.content:
K8s KUBE2IAM vs IRSA - Restrict Pod Permissions To Access AWS Resources

Why Use IRSA or kube2iam in Kubernetes on AWS?

In Kubernetes environments hosted on AWS, securely managing IAM (Identity and Access Management) credentials for pods is important. Without proper management, pods may inadvertently inherit node role permissions, potentially granting them unrestricted access across AWS resources. This scenario highlights the critical need for tools like IRSA (IAM Role for Service Accounts) and kube2iam.

What Happens If We Don’t Use IRSA or Kube2iam?If IRSA or Kube2iam is not implemented:

Pods inherit node role permissions:Pods running in the Kubernetes cluster will default to assuming the IAM role associated with the underlying EC2 instance (node role). This role often has broad permissions, potentially granting unrestricted access to AWS resources not required for the pod.This lack of isolation can lead to security vulnerabilities and compliance issues, as pods might gain unintended access beyond their required scope.

Utilizing IRSA or kube2iam ensures that pods assume only the necessary IAM roles, following the principle of least privilege and enhancing the overall security posture of Kubernetes applications on AWS

o Each pod/deployment can have its own role assigned.o Dynamic IAM role resolution based on pod annotations.

o Flexibility in managing IAM roles and policies for individual pods.

o No need of maintain and use SA.

o Code changes on each pod/deployment

o Requires comprehensive changes to Helm templates by development teams.o Introduces a performance overhead by intercepting AWS API requests.

o Need to have kube2iam configured as DemonSet in the EKS cluster.

o Adds complexity in managing multiple IAM roles and policies.

IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts)

o No code change required if using the existing microservice-authentication Service Account (SA), only annotations must be updated with IAM role.o Integration with AWS IAM roles, providing secure and scalable access control.

o Utilizes native AWS IAM functionalities, reducing the need for additional components.

o Using a single existing SA can be less secure as all platforms will share the same permissions.o Requires careful planning to ensure that IAM roles and policies are efficiently managed.

o Each pod can only be associated with a single IAM role, which may require combining multiple permissions into one role.

Restrict Resources Access: IRSA or Kube2iam

Limitations and Considerations of IRSA in Kubernetes:

  1. Single Service Account Association: A pod can only be associated with a single Kubernetes service account (SA) at a time. Each pod inherits IAM permissions from only one IAM role associated with its SA, limiting direct assignment of multiple SAs to a single pod.
  2. Achieve granular access control by creating separate IAM roles and associating each role with a distinct Kubernetes service account. For different sets of AWS resources, create specific IAM roles and SAs. For example, one IAM role for S3 and OpenSearch, and another for MSK, S3, and DocumentDB.
  3. IAM Role Size Limitations: Each IAM role has a maximum size limit for attached policies. Manage permissions efficiently by grouping related resources and ensuring policies do not exceed size limits.
  4. IAM roles and SAs are scoped within a Kubernetes namespace. Plan IAM role and SA configurations based on namespace requirements and pod-specific permissions within that namespace.
  5. Pod Security and Best Practices: Avoid over-assigning permissions to IAM roles associated with SAs. Follow the principle of least privilege, regularly review IAM policies, and adhere to security best practices to minimize attack surfaces.
  6. Complexity in SA Management:Consideration: Managing multiple SA and IAM Roles/policies can become complex as the number of applications and services growsIncreased complexity might lead to misconfigurations or security loopholes.aintain clear documentation and access control strategies.

If your applications already use any SA,If we want to use the same SA we need to add annotations with the role. like wise if your applications doesnt have any Static SA, we can add the label

  1. Set up an OIDC provider for your EKS cluster:
export CLUSTER_NAME=your-cluster-nameeksctl utils associate-iam-oidc-provider --cluster $CLUSTER_NAME --approve

2. Retrieve the OIDC provider URL:

export OIDC_PROVIDER=$(aws eks describe-cluster --name $CLUSTER_NAME --query "cluster.identity.oidc.issuer" --output text | sed -e "s/^https:\/\///")

Implementing IRSA — IAM Roles, Service Accounts, and Pods:

If Pod needs only OpenSearch and S3 permissions we need to have an IAM Role with S3 and OpenSearch Service

  1. Create an IAM policy for OpenSearch and S3 access:
{    "Version": "2012-10-17",        {            "Action": [                "es:*"            "Resource": "*"    ]

2. Create an IAM Node Role [eks-irsa-s3-opensearch-role] and attach the policy to a IAM Role.

eks-irsa-s3-opensearch-role

Create a Trust relationship, with following code. Replace the OIDC

  "Statement": [      "Effect": "Allow",        "Federated": "arn:aws:iam::'"$ACCOUNT_ID"':oidc-provider/'"$OIDC_PROVIDER"'"      "Action": "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity",        "StringEquals": {
          "'"$OIDC_PROVIDER":aud"'": "sts.amazonaws.com",
          "'"$OIDC_PROVIDER":sub"'": "system:serviceaccount:default:irsa-s3-opensearch-sa"      }  ]

3. Create a ServiceAccount [] to use the above IAM Role [eks-irsa-s3-opensearch-role]

apiVersion: v1kind: ServiceAccount  name: irsa-s3-opensearch-sa  annotations:
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::xxxxxx:role/eks-irsa-s3-opensearch-role

4. Use SA in POD Deployment

apiVersion: v1kind: Pod  name: pod-with-s3-opensearch-accessspec:
  serviceAccountName: irsa-s3-opensearch-sa
  containers:      image: nginx

If Pod needs only MSK, S3 and DocumentDB permissions we need to have a IAM Role with S3 and OpenSearch Service

  1. Follow similar steps as in Scenario 1, adjusting the policy
{    "Version": "2012-10-17",        {            "Action": [                "s3:*",            ],        }}

2. Create a new IAM Node Role [eks-irsa-msk-s3-docudb-role] which are required for pod access and attach the Step 1 Policy and Trust Relation ship.

eks-irsa-msk-s3-docudb-role

2. Create a ServiceAccount [] to use above IAM Role [eks-irsa-s3-opensearch-role]

Create a ns as we are setting up now in Test NS

apiVersion: v1kind: ServiceAccount  name: irsa-msk-s3-docudb-sa  annotations:
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxxx:role/eks-irsa-msk-s3-docudb-role

3. Use SA in POD Deployment

apiVersion: v1kind: Pod  name: pod-with-msk-s3-docudb-accessspec:
  serviceAccountName: irsa-msk-s3-docudb-sa
  containers:      image: nginx

Limitations and Considerations of Using kube2iam

  1. Single IAM Role Per Pod:Limitation: A pod can only be associated with a single IAM role via annotations. You cannot directly assign multiple IAM roles to a single pod. Each pod can inherit permissions from only one IAM role specified in its annotations. Carefully design your IAM roles to combine necessary permissions or split workloads across multiple pods, each with its specific IAM role.
  2. Security Considerations:Limitation: If a pod is compromised, the attacker could potentially access all the resources that the associated IAM role permits. Ensure that the IAM roles grant the minimum necessary permissions. Follow the principle of least privilege, regularly review IAM policies, and rotate IAM credentials.
  3. IAM Role Size Limits:Consideration: Each IAM role has a maximum size limit for attached policies. Ensure your policies do not exceed these limits. Combine permissions efficiently to fit within a single role if necessary.
  4. Namespace Scope and Isolation:Consideration: kube2iam annotations are pod/deployment. Ensure roles are appropriately scoped within namespaces and that sensitive roles are not overly permissive across namespaces. Use separate namespaces to isolate environments and apply specific IAM roles to each namespace.
  5. Performance Overhead:Consideration: kube2iam introduces a performance overhead by intercepting and processing AWS API requests. This could impact the performance of applications that make frequent AWS API calls. Monitor the performance and scalability of kube2iam in your cluster and adjust resource limits as needed.
  6. Complexity in Role Management:Consideration: Managing multiple IAM roles and policies can become complex as the number of applications and services grows. Increased complexity might lead to misconfigurations or security loopholes. Maintain clear documentation and access control strategies.
  1. When a pod with a Kube2IAM annotation makes an AWS API call, the AWS SDK (or CLI) attempts to retrieve credentials from the AWS metadata service (http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/).
  2. Kube2IAM intercepts this request using iptables rules.
  3. Kube2IAM, running on the node, the IAM role specified in the pod’s annotation using the node’s IAM role (node_instance_role).
  4. Kube2IAM then returns temporary credentials for the assumed role to the pod.

Example Code for implementing Kube2IAM: Prerequisites:

  1. Need to have IAM Roles created for specific services and update the trusts to have node roles to assume
  2. Need to have Kube2IAM Demonset deployed

Purpose: Grants the pod permissions to access OpenSearch.

{    "Version": "2012-10-17",        {            "Action": [                "es:ListDomainNames",
                "es:DescribeElasticsearchDomain",
                "es:ListElasticsearchDomainNames",                "es:ESHttpPut",                "es:ESHttpDelete"            "Resource": "arn:aws:es:YOUR_REGION:YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID:domain/YOUR_OPENSEARCH_DOMAIN/*"    ]
  • Replace YOUR_REGION, YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID, and YOUR_OPENSEARCH_DOMAIN with your actual values.
  • Tighten the Action list if possible. Instead of es:ESHttpGet, etc., specify the API calls your application makes.
  • The Resource ARN should be as specific as possible.
  • Trust Relationship: (As previously documented, but repeating for completeness). This allows the EC2 instances (your Kubernetes nodes) to this role.
{    "Version": "2012-10-17",        {            "Principal": {
                "Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com"
            },
            "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
        },            "Effect": "Allow",                "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID:role/YOUR_NODE_INSTANCE_ROLE"            "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"    ]

Replace YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID and YOUR_NODE_INSTANCE_ROLE with your actual values. YOUR_NODE_INSTANCE_ROLE is the IAM role assigned to your EC2 worker nodes.

  • Purpose: Grants the pod permissions to access OpenSearch and S3.
{    "Version": "2012-10-17",        {            "Action": [                "es:ListDomainNames",
                "es:DescribeElasticsearchDomain",
                "es:ListElasticsearchDomainNames",                "es:ESHttpPut",                "es:ESHttpDelete"            "Resource": "arn:aws:es:YOUR_REGION:YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID:domain/YOUR_OPENSEARCH_DOMAIN/*"        {            "Action": [                "s3:GetObject",                "s3:DeleteObject"            "Resource": [
                "arn:aws:s3:::YOUR_BUCKET",
                "arn:aws:s3:::YOUR_BUCKET/*"        }}
  • Replace YOUR_REGION, YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID, and YOUR_OPENSEARCH_DOMAIN with your actual values for the OpenSearch section.
  • Replace YOUR_BUCKET with your S3 bucket name(s). If you need access to multiple buckets, add them as separate Resource entries.
  • Again, tighten the Action list to the minimum required permissions. If the pod only from S3, remove s3:PutObject and s3:DeleteObject.
  • Trust Relationship: Identical to k2iam-opensearch-role. It allows the EC2 instances (your Kubernetes nodes) to this role.
{    "Version": "2012-10-17",        {            "Principal": {
                "Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com"
            },
            "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
        },            "Effect": "Allow",                "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID:role/YOUR_NODE_INSTANCE_ROLE"            "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"    ]

We can have One Default role which we can add all required services in one common IAM Role 

{    "Version": "2012-10-17",        {            "Action": "sts:AssumeRole",                "arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxx:role/k2iam-opensearch-role",
                "arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxx:role/k2iam-docudb-role",
                "arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxx:role/k2iam-s3-role"        },            "Effect": "Allow",                "ec2:DescribeInstances",            ],        }}

IAM Role’s Trust relationships:

#### Trust for node roles#### Replace Node IAM Role within the EKS cluster where pod runs     "Version": "2012-10-17",        {            "Principal": {
                "Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com"
            },
            "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
        },            "Effect": "Allow",                "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxx:role/NodeInstanceRole-HdShDP"            "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"    ]
Configure KUBE2IAM :Let's configure kube2iam with example, This will create a Demonset resources which will be running on all the nodes where pods get deployed so kube2iam intercepts AWS API requests originating from pods within the Kubernetes cluster.

: Let’s configure kube2iam with example, This will create a Demonset resources which will be running on all the nodes where pods get deployed So kube2iam intercepts AWS API requests originating from pods within the Kubernetes cluster.

— — base-role-arn=arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxx:role/ — — default-role=k2iam-default

# Source: kube2iam/templates/serviceaccount.yamlkind: ServiceAccount  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: kube2iam
    app.kubernetes.io/instance: kube2iam---
# Source: kube2iam/templates/clusterrole.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1metadata:    app.kubernetes.io/name: kube2iam
    app.kubernetes.io/instance: kube2iam
  name: kube2iam- apiGroups:  resources:  - pods  - list  - get# Source: kube2iam/templates/clusterrolebinding.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: kube2iam
    app.kubernetes.io/instance: kube2iamroleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRolesubjects:  name: kube2iam---
# Source: kube2iam/templates/daemonset.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1metadata:    app.kubernetes.io/instance: kube2iam
    app.kubernetes.io/name: kube2iam
  name: kube2iamspec:    matchLabels:
      app.kubernetes.io/instance: kube2iam
      app.kubernetes.io/name: kube2iam    metadata:        app.kubernetes.io/instance: kube2iam
        app.kubernetes.io/name: kube2iam
    spec:      - args:        - --node=$(NODE_NAME)        - --iptables=true
        - --base-role-arn=arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxx:role/
        - --default-role=k2iam-default        - --metrics-port=8181
        - --use-regional-sts-endpoint
        - --verbose        - name: AWS_REGION        - name: HOST_IP            fieldRef:              fieldPath: status.podIP          valueFrom:              apiVersion: v1        image: jtblin/kube2iam:0.11.2
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
        livenessProbe:          httpGet:            port: 8181          initialDelaySeconds: 30          successThreshold: 1        name: kube2iam        - containerPort: 8181          protocol: TCP        securityContext:        terminationMessagePath: /dev/termination-log
        terminationMessagePolicy: File
      dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst      restartPolicy: Always
      schedulerName: default-scheduler
      securityContext: {}      serviceAccountName: kube2iam
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30
  updateStrategy:

Copy the above code to a file (kube2iam.yaml) and you can run the below commands. If you want the pods to be running on the specific node you can use the concept of taints/tolerents

kubectl apply -f kube2iam.yaml -n kube-system
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep kube2iam 
#pods will be up and running, no of pods depending on the nodes

Example Code for implementing Kube2IAM — IAM Roles, Pods

Scenario 1: If Pod needs only Opensearch and S3 permissions we need to have a IAM Role with Opensearch

  1. Need to have IAM Roles created for specific services Opensearch and update the trusts to have node roles to assume
  2. Need to have Kube2IAM Demonset deployed

Annotate the Pod with the IAM Role

Create a Kubernetes pod with the annotation specifying the IAM role (kube2iam-s3-role) for Opensearch access.

apiVersion: v1kind: Pod  name: services-pod-opensearch  annotations:
    iam.amazonaws.com/role: k2iam-opensearch-role
spec:    - name: aws-cli-test-kube2iam
      image: amazon/aws-cli:2.11.13
      command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]        - "while true; do sleep 30; done"        - name: AWS_REGION
          value: us-east-1  # Replace with your AWS region
      securityContext:
        allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      resources:          cpu: 0.5        requests:          memory: 256Mi

Once we exec into the pod we can check the iam role associated to the pod

kubectl exec -it   services-test-pod-few  -n test-kube2iam -- bashbash-4.2# aws sts get-caller-identity    "UserId": "xxxxxx:xxxxxxx-kiam-opensearch-role",    "Arn": "arn:aws:sts::xxxxxx:assumed-role/k2iam-opensearch-role/xxxxxx-kube2iam-opensearch-role"bash-4.2# curl -u xxxxx:xxxxx "https://opensearch.amazonaws.com"  "name" : "xxx",
  "cluster_name" : "xxxxxxxxx:xxxxxxx",
  "cluster_uuid" : "xxxxxx",    "distribution" : "opensearch",    "build_type" : "tar",
    "build_snapshot" : false,
    "lucene_version" : "9.7.0"  "tagline" : "The OpenSearch Project: https://opensearch.org/"An error occurred (AccessDenied) when calling the ListBuckets operation: Access Denied

If you see when I tried to list s3 buckets, I got AccessDenied. The core issue here is that the k2iam-opensearch-role has permissions for OpenSearch. Therefore, when you tried aws s3 ls, you correctly received an AccessDenied error. This confirms that Kube2IAM is working as configured!

Explanation: The pod assumed the k2iam-opensearch-role via the annotation. That role's policy didn't include S3 permissions.

Scenario 2: If Pod needs only Opensearch and S3 permissionswe need to have a IAM Role with with Opensearch and s3 access binded to the role

Annotate the Pod with the IAM Role

  1. Need to have IAM Roles created for specific services Opensearch and s3 and update the trusts to have node roles to assume
  2. Need to have Kube2IAM Demonset deployed

IAM Role with multiple Services

  1. k2iam-opensearch-s3-role: IAM Role created, with policy having s3 and opensearch service

Annotate the Pod with the IAM Role

Create a Kubernetes pod with the annotation specifying the IAM role (k2iam-opensearch-s3-role) for Opensearch access.

apiVersion: v1metadata:
  name: services-test-pod-s3-os
  namespace: test    iam.amazonaws.com/role: k2iam-opensearch-s3-role  containers:
    - name: aws-cli-test-kube2iam
      image: amazon/aws-cli:2.11.13
      command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
      args:
        - "while true; do sleep 30; done"
      env:          value: us-east-1  # Replace with your AWS region        allowPrivilegeEscalation: false        limits:          memory: 512Mi          cpu: 0.25  restartPolicy: Never

Once we exec into the pod we can check the iam role associated to the pod

kubectl exec -it services-test-pod-s3-os -n test-kube2iam -- bash
bash-4.2#  aws sts get-caller-identity
{
    "UserId": "xxxxxx:xxxxxx-k2iam-opensearch-s3-role",
    "Account": "xxxxx",
    "Arn": "arn:aws:sts::xxxxxx:assumed-role/k2iam-opensearch-s3-role/xxxxxx-kube2iam-opensearch-s3-role"
}curl -u xxxxxx:xxxxxxx "https://xxxxxxxx.es.amazonaws.com"  "name" : "xxxxx",
  "cluster_name" : "xxxxxxxx:xxxxxxxx",
  "cluster_uuid" : "xxxxxxxxxx",    "distribution" : "opensearch",    "build_type" : "tar",
    "build_snapshot" : false,
    "lucene_version" : "9.7.0",
    "minimum_wire_compatibility_version" : "7.10.0",
    "minimum_index_compatibility_version" : "7.0.0"  "tagline" : "The OpenSearch Project: https://opensearch.org/"
bash-4.2# aws s3 ls | grep test-int
2021-08-26 13:28:21 test-int

In this scenario, the k2iam-opensearch-s3-role had permissions for both OpenSearch and S3. The aws s3 ls command succeeded, demonstrating that the pod could access S3.

items.title: How to Run Axivion Dashboard Server in Docker (Step-by-Step Guide)
items.author: Abu Bakkar Siddique
items.content:
In this blog, I’ll walk you through how to containerize and run Axivion Bauhaus Suite using Docker, providing easy access to its dashboard portal directly from your local machine.

  • Docker Desktop installed (Windows/Linux/macOS)
  • Axivion Bauhaus Suite tar file (bauhaus-suite-*.tar.gz)
  • Axivion license key (*.key)

Step 1: Prepare Your Files

Create a clean directory on your local machine and ensure it contains:

  • Axivion Bauhaus Suite Linux setup (bauhaus-suite-<version>-x86_64-gnu_linux.tar.gz)

Step 2: Create Dockerfile

Use the following Dockerfile to set up your environment:

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \  gcc \  make \  python3 \  python3-openpyxl \  apt-get clean
RUN useradd -ms /bin/bash axivionUSER axivion
COPY bauhaus-suite-*-x86_64-gnu_linux.tar.gz .COPY *.key .
RUN mkdir bauhaus-suite && \    tar -xzf bauhaus-suite-*-x86_64-gnu_linux.tar.gz -C bauhaus-suite --strip-components=1 && \
    cp *.key bauhaus-suite/config/
RUN chmod +x /home/axivion/bauhaus-suite/setup.sh && \    /home/axivion/bauhaus-suite/setup.sh --non-interactive && \
ENV PATH="/home/axivion/bauhaus-suite/bin:${PATH}"
WORKDIR /home/axivion/bauhaus-suite/dashboard
ENV AXIVION_DASHBOARD_CONFIG="/home/axivion/bauhaus-suite/dashboard"
ENTRYPOINT ["bash", "-c", "dashserver start && tail -f /dev/null"]

Step 3: Build Your Docker Image

Run the following command in the directory containing your Dockerfile:

docker build -t axivion-dashboard .

Step 3: Run Docker Container

Launch your Axivion dashboard container:

docker run -d -p 9443:9443 --name axivion-dashboard axivion-dashboard

Step 4: Access the Axivion Dashboard

Open your web browser and visit:

https://localhost:9443/axivion/

You’ll likely encounter a security warning since the certificate is self-signed. Proceed past this warning to access your dashboard.

Step 4: Login to Axivion Dashboard

Check container logs to obtain the default admin credentials:

docker logs axivion-dashboard

You’ll find something like:

Username: adminPassword: [auto-generated password]

Log in with these credentials and update your password immediately upon first login.

That’s it! You’ve successfully containerized the Axivion Bauhaus Suite Dashboard, allowing easy management and deployment directly through Docker.

Enjoy your seamless Axivion environment setup!

items.title: Take control of your code with Amazon Q Developer’s new context features
items.author: Brian Beach
items.content:
In this blog post, I dive into the powerful new features of Amazon Q Developer that empower developers to take full control of their development workflow. These features, currently available in Visual Studio Code, allow you to leverage workspace context, explicit context, prompt libraries, and project rules to streamline your software projects, maintain coding standards, and boost your overall productivity. Developers working in other IDEs can expect support for these capabilities to come soon as well. Whether you’re an experienced developer or just starting out, these features will transform the way you approach your development tasks.

For the past year, Amazon Q Developer has supported workspace context in the integrated development environment (IDE). This powerful feature allows Amazon Q Developer to automatically ingest and index all your code files, configurations, and project structure, giving the AI-powered assistant comprehensive context across your entire application.

By adding the modifier to your question, Amazon Q Developer can automatically include the most relevant chunks of your workspace code as additional context. This allows the assistant to provide more thorough and accurate responses, even for questions that require understanding the broader codebase, rather than just the current file.

To illustrate this, I will use code from the AWS CDK Immersion Day Workshop. In the following example, I ask Amazon Q Developer, “Which resources are deployed by the workshop stack?” Typically, the assistant would only have the current file open in the IDE as context to answer this question. However, by adding , Amazon Q Developer can include additional files to provide a more complete response, describing all the resources in the project, such as AWS Lambda functions, an Amazon API Gateway, and an Amazon DynamoDB table.

With this additional context, Amazon Q Developer can provide a more comprehensive answer, explaining the various resources that make up the workshop stack.

Amazon Q Developer can’t review every file in a large project–that would take too long. The assistant determines relevance based on an index that is updated periodically. While this works great in most cases, there are times when I want to know which files Amazon Q picked. The new context transparency feature, gives me the insight I need.

Amazon Q Developer now includes the context information directly in the response, listing each file that was added. The context transparency feature allows me to see exactly which files the assistant used to formulate its answer. In the following example, you can see that Q Developer included four files from my project.

By expanding the context section, you can easily see the files that Amazon Q Developer used to provide the previous response, giving you greater insight into the assistant’s decision-making process.

While Amazon Q Developer typically does a great job of picking the best files to augment the context, there are times when I want more control. The new explicit context feature allows me to choose individual files and folders that I want to add to the context.

By typing in the chat, Amazon Q Developer displays a user interface that lets you select the specific files and folders you want to include. This provides the assistant with access to the exact information that you know it needs to answer your question.

Let’s return to the prior example. While Q Developer properly identified all the resources in the stack, it did not include where the DynamoDB table is defined. While the answer is correct, I want Q. Developer to see the source of the . In the following example, I explicitly add the folder to the context because I know that is where the resources are defined.

Rather than allowing Q to choose the files, I can explicitly identify files and folders that I want to add to the context. In the following image you see that Q again answered my question correctly. However, with the source code to both files, Q was able to include additional information missing from the prior example. Notice that the now includes additional information about the runtime and version.

With the ability to explicitly define the context, I can tailor the information Amazon Q Developer uses to provide the most relevant and accurate response for my specific needs.

In addition to controlling the context, Amazon Q Developer now allows you to build a library of common prompts. These prompts are stored as Markdown files in my folder, making it easy to reuse them across multiple conversations and projects.

For example, let’s say you want to add a diagram to the README file for your project. While Amazon Q Developer’s feature can already generate an infrastructure diagram, you may have other types of diagrams, like an Entity-Relationship (ER) diagram or a sequence diagram, that you use frequently. By storing these prompts in your library, you can easily add them to the context without having to retype the prompt each time. In the following example, I will create a sequence diagram.

In the prior image, you can see that I have again added the folder to the context in addition to a saved prompt named “Create Sequence Diagram”. Note that I can combine multiple modifiers adding additional context and prompts as needed. The stored prompt for “Create Sequence Diagram” is shown below.

Create a sequence diagram using Mermaid that shows the sequence of calls between resources. 
Ignore supporting resources like IAM policies and security group rules.

Amazon Q Developer uses the prompt, and the two source code files in the folder to create the following sequence diagram.

The prompt library saves me time and reduces the chances of making mistakes when generating common artifacts, allowing me to focus on more complex and creative aspects of my development workflow.

The last feature we’ll explore is “Project Rules.” Similar to the prompt library, these rules are stored as Markdown files. Unlike the prompt library, which is specific to your user profile, project rules are stored in the folder of your project. Therfore, they are applied to all developers that share the project source code.

These rules allow you to enforce coding standards and best practices across your team. For example, you could have a rule that all Python code uses type hints, or that all Java code uses Javadocs comments. By storing these rules in your project, you can drive consistency across developers, regardless of their experience level.

To illustrate this, imagine that a junior developer asks Q to “Add an S3 bucket to the stack” as shown in the following image. This prompt is too simple and does not include our best practices.

Despite the vague prompt, you might notice that Q Developer’s response mentions the “best practices” stored in “the rules file”. You might also notice that Q has added to the context even though I did not include any modifiers in my prompt. This is the power of the project rule. It adds additional context to the prompt, even when the developer forgets to add it manually. includes the following text. In addition, context transparency makes it clear that the rule was included so the developer is aware.

All S3 Buckets must have encryption enabled, enforce SSL, and block public access. 
All DynamoDB Tables must have encryption enabled.
All SNS Topics must have encryption enabled and enforce SSL.
All SQS Queues must enforce SSL.

Project rules empower you to maintain consistency and best practices across your entire development team, helping to improve the overall quality and maintainability of your codebase.

The new features in Amazon Q Developer give you powerful tools to simplify your software development workflow. By leveraging workspace context, explicit context, prompt libraries, and project rules, you can ensure your AI assistant has the information it needs to provide accurate and helpful responses, while also enforcing best practices and standards across your team.

items.title: DeepSeek-R1 now available as a fully managed serverless model in Amazon Bedrock
items.author: Channy Yun (윤석찬)
items.content:
The fully managed DeepSeek-R1 model is now generally available in Amazon Bedrock. Amazon Web Services (AWS) is the first cloud service provider (CSP) to deliver DeepSeek-R1 as a fully managed, generally available model. You can accelerate innovation and deliver tangible business value with DeepSeek on AWS without having to manage infrastructure complexities. You can power your generative AI applications with DeepSeek-R1’s capabilities using a single API in the Amazon Bedrock’s fully managed service and get the benefit of its extensive features and tooling.

According to DeepSeek, their model is publicly available under MIT license and offers strong capabilities in reasoning, coding, and natural language understanding. These capabilities power intelligent decision support, software development, mathematical problem-solving, scientific analysis, data insights, and comprehensive knowledge management systems.

As is the case for all AI solutions, give careful consideration to data privacy requirements when implementing in your production environments, check for bias in output, and monitor your results. When implementing publicly available models like DeepSeek-R1, consider the following:

  • – You can access the enterprise-grade security, monitoring, and cost control features of Amazon Bedrock that are essential for deploying AI responsibly at scale, all while retaining complete control over your data. Users’ inputs and model outputs aren’t shared with any model providers. You can use these key security features by default, including data encryption at rest and in transit, fine-grained access controls, secure connectivity options, and download various compliance certifications while communicating with the DeepSeek-R1 model in Amazon Bedrock.
  • – You can implement safeguards customized to your application requirements and responsible AI policies with Amazon Bedrock Guardrails. This includes key features of content filtering, sensitive information filtering, and customizable security controls to prevent hallucinations using contextual grounding and Automated Reasoning checks. This means you can control the interaction between users and the DeepSeek-R1 model in Bedrock with your defined set of policies by filtering undesirable and harmful content in your generative AI applications.
  • – You can evaluate and compare models to identify the optimal model for your use case, including DeepSeek-R1, in a few steps through either automatic or human evaluations by using Amazon Bedrock model evaluation tools. You can choose automatic evaluation with predefined metrics such as accuracy, robustness, and toxicity. Alternatively, you can choose human evaluation workflows for subjective or custom metrics such as relevance, style, and alignment to brand voice. Model evaluation provides built-in curated datasets, or you can bring in your own datasets.

Get started with the DeepSeek-R1 model in Amazon Bedrock If you’re new to using DeepSeek-R1 models, go to the Amazon Bedrock console, choose under in the left navigation pane. To access the fully managed DeepSeek-R1 model, request access for in . You’ll then be granted access to the model in Amazon Bedrock.

Next, to test the DeepSeek-R1 model in Amazon Bedrock, choose under in the left menu pane. Then choose in the upper left, and select as the category and as the model. Then choose .

Using the selected model, I run the following prompt example:

A family has $5,000 to save for their vacation next year. They can place the money in a savings account earning 2% interest annually or in a certificate of deposit earning 4% interest annually but with no access to the funds until the vacation. If they need $1,000 for emergency expenses during the year, how should they divide their money between the two options to maximize their vacation fund?

This prompt requires a complex chain of thought and produces very precise reasoning results.

Here is a sample of the AWS CLI command:

aws bedrock-runtime invoke-model \
       --model-id us.deepseek.r1-v1:0 \
       --body "{\"prompt\": \"<｜begin_of_sentence｜><｜User｜>Type_Your_Prompt_Here<｜Assistant｜><think>\n\", \"max_tokens\": 512, \"temperature\": 0.5, \"top_p\": 0.9}" \
       --cli-binary-format raw-in-base64-out \
       --region us-west-2 \
       invoke-model-output.txt
import boto3
from botocore.exceptions import ClientError

# Create a Bedrock Runtime client in the AWS Region you want to use.
client = boto3.client("bedrock-runtime", region_name="us-west-2")

# Set the model ID, e.g., DeepSeek-R1 Model.
model_id = "us.deepseek.r1-v1:0"

# Start a conversation with the user message.
user_message = "Type_Your_Prompt_Here"
conversation = [
    {
        "role": "user",
        "content": [{"text": user_message}],
    }
]

try:
    # Send the message to the model, using a basic inference configuration.
    response = client.converse(
        modelId=model_id,
        messages=conversation,
        inferenceConfig={"maxTokens": 512, "temperature": 0.5, "topP": 0.9},
    )

    # Extract and print the response text.
    response_text = response["output"]["message"]["content"][0]["text"]
    print(response_text)

except (ClientError, Exception) as e:
    print(f"ERROR: Can't invoke '{model_id}'. Reason: {e}")
    exit(1)

To enable Amazon Bedrock Guardrails on the DeepSeek-R1 model, select under in the left navigation pane, and create a guardrail by configuring as many filters as you need. For example, if you filter for “politics” word, your guardrails will recognize this word in the prompt and show you the blocked message.

You can test the guardrail with different inputs to assess the guardrail’s performance. You can refine the guardrail by setting denied topics, word filters, sensitive information filters, and blocked messaging until it matches your needs.

Here’s a demo walkthrough highlighting how you can take advantage of the fully managed DeepSeek-R1 model in Amazon Bedrock:
